Hmm: router input is rmsnorm(h) after attn residual. So MoE phase: - Phase A: router GEMV + softmax + topk (all CTAs compute redundantly — 294KB read from L2, ~cheap; or CTA0 does it and others spin on a flag...). Then expert gate/up GEMV: 9 experts × 2048 outs = 18432 cols... but expert selection depends on topk which all CTAs compute identically → no extra sync needed if all CTAs do the router+topk redundantly! Nice: each CTA computes router logits (64 outs, needs full rmsnorm(h) — 2304 reads + 294KB weights from L2), softmax, top8, gets identical expert ids and weights. Then grid-stride over selected (expert, out_col_chunk) work items for gate+up fused GEMV computing silu(g)*(u) into scratch (9×1024). - B4 - MoE phase 2: down GEMV per expert: 1024→2304 × 9, weighted sum → need sum over experts of w_e * down_e. Parallel by out cols; each CTA computes for its out-col slice the full sum over 9 experts (reads scratch h_e (1024 bf16 each) — from L2, and down weights slice for each expert: 9 × 1024×(2304/P) int4). Weight traffic per CTA: its out slice × 1024 × 9 experts /2 bytes. Total = 9×1024×2304/2 = 10.6MB, matches. Add residual → x_{i+1}. Write to global. - B5 → next block. So KDA block: barriers: B1 (after qkvg GEMV), B2 (after conv/S), B3 (after o_proj+residual, before MoE since router needs full h... wait MoE phase1 needs rmsnorm(h) which needs full h from phase o_proj → yes barrier B3), B4 (between gate/up and down). Then next block's phase 0 needs x from MoE down (B5). Total 4-5 barriers/block. Hmm wait, can o_proj GEMV and residual be fused with rmsnorm? Each CTA computes a slice of the o_proj outputs + residual → partial h. rmsnorm needs the mean-square over the full h. Could compute partial sums via atomics then barrier anyway. Not saving barriers. Alternative: Instead of global barriers between ALL CTAs, note that phases don't need ALL work done before the next phase starts — e.g., conv phase for head h only needs q/k/v cols [h*128, (h+1)*128) done. But simplest correct thing: full barrier. Barrier cost estimate: grid ~188 CTAs (persistent, 1 CTA/SM at a time? we want max occupancy probably 2+ CTAs per SM... but barriers require ALL CTAs co-resident). Let me think about the barrier mechanism: an atomic counter per phase; each CTA arrives: old = atomicAdd(count,1); if old == grid-1 { set flag = phase; reset... } Classic two-counter or sense-reversing barrier. Polling with ld.acquire on a global flag: latency ~ 1µs? Actually in practice, device-wide sync via cooperative groups is implemented similarly. Cost estimates: with 188 CTAs, barrier latency is dominated by the last arrival + flag propagation ≈ 0.5-2µs. Total barriers ~ 4 blocks × 5 = 20 → 10-40µs. Hmm. That's meaningful vs ~130µs budget but acceptable. But actually we can be smarter: barrier only needs participation of CTAs... it's global anyway. Actually wait — maybe better: can we do fewer, bigger phases with different parallelization? Let me reconsider. The minimum data-dependency phases per block: 1. attn input GEMVs (needs x, rmsnorm computed inline per CTA) 2. attn core (conv/S update or MLA attention) → o vector 3. o_proj + residual → h 4. MoE router→selected gate/up GEMV (needs full h for router, and rmsnorm(h)) 5. down + combine + residual → x_next 5 phases → 4 barriers per block (between phases) + 1 barrier at block boundary... same thing: 4 barriers/block × 4 = 16 barriers. Estimate worst case 2µs each = 32µs. Plus actual work ~130µs. Total ~160µs vs baseline. What's the baseline latency? Let's estimate: baseline materializes each weight (int4 read + bf16 write + bf16 read) = 9× weight bytes traffic. Weight bytes (int4) ≈ 213MB → baseline traffic ≈ 213 + 2×(4×213) = 213MB int4 + 426MB unpacked-write +... let me just carefully: baseline per weight: read int4 (B bytes), write unpacked uint8 K×N (2B), read it, compute dequant in bf16 (write 4B bytes bf16 = 4B, read back 4B for matmul)... roughly traffic ≈ B + 2B + 2B + 4B + 4B = 13B?? Actually torch does: wu = unpack → uint8 tensor (write 2B, read 2B for sub/cast), (wu - z)*s → bf16 tensor (write 4B), then matmul reads bf16 (4B). So total ≈ read int4 B + write uint8 2B + read uint8 2B + write bf16 4B + read bf16 4B = 13B where B = int4 bytes ≈ 213MB → ~2.7GB!? No wait, that overcounts: unpack does read packed + write unpacked. dequant reads unpacked + scales + writes bf16. matmul reads bf16. So traffic = B (int4 read) + 2B (uint8 write) + ~2B (uint8 read) + 4B (bf16 write) + 4B (bf16 read) = 13B ≈ 2.8GB → at 1.8TB/s ≈ 1.55ms plus kernel overheads. Plus MLA materialization: kvb = c_kv @ kv_b for L=16384: reads 18.9MB cache + 2.1MB int4 (in materialized form 4.2M×... kv_b bf16 = 8192×512×2 = 8.4MB) writes L×8192 bf16 = 16384×8192×2 = 268MB, reads it back for einsum (268MB) → 555MB extra + attention scores etc. So baseline at ctx=16k probably ~2.5-3ms. With many small kernels: 3 blocks... per step maybe ~200 kernel launches... anyway baseline is slow. Our target: ~150-180µs → ~10-15× speedup. Geomean similar at 2048 (smaller MLA cache effect, but weights dominate everywhere: baseline ~2.8GB traffic regardless of ctx; our ~220MB + cache read L×576×2 = 2.4MB@2048). So speedup ~10-18× roughly, if we're bandwidth-efficient. OK so the plan: Triton persistent megakernel with software barriers. Key question: can Triton do a reliable global barrier? Yes — atomics in Triton: tl.atomic_add on a scalar pointer, and spin with `while tl.atomic_add(flag, 0) < target: pass`? Triton supports while loops... Actually spin loops in Triton are possible: ``` tl.atomic_add(bar_ptr, 1, sem="acq_rel") count = tl.atomic_add(bar_ptr, 0, sem="acquire") # or ld while count < target: tl.atomic_add(bar_ptr, 0) # hmm, spin ``` Hmm, need care: all CTAs increment counter when arriving; last one to arrive sees count==grid, resets a phase flag; others spin on a separate "release" flag. Sense reversal per barrier index to avoid reset races: barrier k uses slot k (pre-allocated array of N barrier slots); each CTA: v = atomic_add(slot[k], 1); if v == grid-1: atomic_xchg(release[k], 1, release); else: spin on ld volatile release[k] until ==1... but next use of slot k? Use one slot per barrier instance (we have ≤ 64 barriers per launch, preallocate 64 int32 slots) — actually per launch the barrier sequence is deterministic; allocate, say, 128 slots, zeroed before launch (that's an extra memset kernel before the launch... not allowed? "the decode step ... must be a SINGLE GPU kernel launch". Zeroing barriers could be done by the LAST user... simpler: barriers state persists across steps; manage phase counting so slots wrap with sense reversal. Or: barrier counts accumulate: slot[k] target = grid * (number of times any CTA passed barrier k total). Since every CTA passes every barrier exactly once per step, count for slot k after step s = grid*(s+1). So spin until slot[k] >= grid*(s+1)... atomics monotonic — no reset needed! Barrier: t = atomic_add(slot_k, 1, sem=release) + 1; spin until ld_acquire(slot_k) >= grid_total_expected where grid_total_expected grows per step... but next step re-uses the kernel with same slots. Pass step_count as arg? The Model can pass a per-call counter. Hmm wait but release ordering: CTA does atomic_add(+1, sem="release") after writing its results; spin with atomic_add(slot, 0, sem="acquire") until >= grid*calls... but CTAs that arrive "early" at barrier k for step s+1 could push count beyond... no: CTAs can't arrive at barrier k for step s+1 before all CTAs passed barrier k at step s (program order within each CTA). So monotonic counting works: at step s (0-based), each CTA does atomic_add → count goes from grid*s to grid*(s+1). Spin until >= grid*(s+1). pass s (or the target) as a kernel arg. But wait — do we even need a release flag separate: just spin on the counter itself with acquire semantics. All writes before atomic_add(release) visible to observers who acquire-read the count ≥ target. That's correct on CUDA with proper memory ordering (Triton atomics with sem="acq_rel"/"acquire", scope="gpu"). Spin loop in Triton: ``` target = grid * (s+1) tl.atomic_add(slot_ptr + k, 1, sem="acq_rel") c = tl.atomic_add(slot_ptr + k, 0, sem="acquire") while c < target: c = tl.atomic_add(slot_ptr + k, 0, sem="acquire") ``` Hmm, atomic_add(ptr, 0) generates atom.add which in a spin loop hammers L2. Better: tl.load with volatile? Triton: `tl.load(ptr, volatile=True)` — hmm, may be cached in L1? volatile prevents caching? In Triton, tl.load(..., volatile=True) generates ld.volatile which bypasses L1? ld.volatile bypasses L1 and goes to L2. OK that works; but need acquire semantics — ld.volatile + memory fence? We can do `tl.atomic_add(slot,0, sem="acquire")` once after volatile spin sees target to establish acquire. That's standard. Actually CUDA ld.volatile ensures visibility of previously-released writes? volatile gives no ordering guarantees officially, but on NVIDIA hardware, system-scope coherence at L2 makes it work in practice with a fence: use `tl.debug_barrier()`? No that's CTA-level. There's tl.atomic_fence? Not in mainline Triton... there's `tl.atomic_add` with sem which emits fence as needed. I'll do: spin: `while tl.load(slot+k, volatile=True) < target: pass` then one `tl.atomic_add(slot+k, 0, sem="acquire")` to order. Alternatively use cooperative grid launch via torch? Triton doesn't expose grid sync. Manual barriers it is. Big risk: co-residency. Grid must be ≤ min concurrent CTAs. If occupancy is limited by smem/regs, grid = SMs × CTAs/SM must hold ALL CTAs resident simultaneously, else deadlock (a non-resident CTA never starts while resident ones spin forever). So grid must be #SMs × (max co-resident CTAs), and we must NOT oversubscribe. Compute it: query device SM count, set smem/regs usage conservatively, launch grid = SMs × C where C is safe (e.g., if kernel uses ≤ 100KB smem and ≤ 128 regs... get C=1 or 2). Simplest: grid = #SMs, 1 CTA/SM, big smem per CTA (say 100KB) → no co-residency risk as long as kernel fits 1 CTA/SM (always true). But underutilizes latency hiding (only 1 CTA of up to 1024-2048 threads per SM... Triton num_warps up to 32? we can use num_warps=8/16). Bandwidth utilization with 188 CTAs × 8 warps: each CTA streams weights; to hit 1.8TB/s we need enough outstanding loads. 188 CTAs × 1024 threads... Triton with num_warps=8 → 188×256 threads = 48K threads; with wide vectorized loads (128-bit) and unrolling, can we saturate? Modern GPUs need ~ enough bytes in flight: 1.8TB/s × ~800ns latency ≈ 1.4MB in flight. With 188 CTAs × 8 warps × 32 threads × 16B = 770KB per "wave" of loads; need 2+ concurrent loads per thread via unrolling → OK plausible. Also L2 and DRAM pipelining helps. Many Triton GEMV kernels saturate bandwidth with grids of a few hundred CTAs. Alternatively 2 CTAs/SM with ≤113KB smem each (Blackwell has 228KB smem/SM). Grid = 376. More parallelism, less per-CTA smem. We'll tune. Now the real question — Triton implementability of all these phases in ONE @triton.jit kernel. It's one kernel function with a big program: use program_id for work assignment, lots of if/else per phase, barriers between. All shapes are static (kernel constexprs) except context length L (runtime). Fine. Per-phase work decomposition — let me define phases and their parallelization assuming grid CTAs indexed pid, each with num_warps=8 (256 threads) maybe 16. Let me define the global scratch buffers (preallocated, passed as pointers): - x_cur (2304 bf16) — current hidden (input token or previous block output) - scratch h (2304 f32 or bf16) post-attn residual - normed (2304 f32/bf16) — rmsnorm output (or computed redundantly per CTA) - qkv buffers: q(4096 f32), k(4096), v(4096), g(4096: log-decay pre computed as exp or raw), beta(32) - o buffer (4096 f32) attention output pre-o_proj - MoE: eh (9×1024 f32) gate*up silu outputs; router logits; expert ids/weights (all CTAs compute redundantly anyway → keep in registers per CTA) - MLA: mla_q (6144), kv (576 new), q_abs (32×512 f32), cache appended, attention partials: per-CTA-chunk m_i/l_i/acc (32×512)... for flash combine: NVARS. alloc: part_m (NCHUNK×32), part_l, part_acc (NCHUNK×32×512 f32 = e.g. 64×32×512×4 = 4MB — fine, global scratch). Hmm wait — flash-style chunking: assign L positions to C chunks; each CTA processes several chunks; writes partial m_i, l_i, acc_i[512 per head]? acc per head is 512 floats → per chunk 32×512×4 = 64KB × (#chunks). With C chunks... accuracy: combine phase reduces across chunks per head. Alternatively two-phase with global atomics for m/l? Messy. Keep chunk partials in scratch. Actually simpler for attention: kv score pass and weighted-sum pass can be one pass with online softmax per CTA over its positions; partial results (m_i, l_i, acc_i) per (CTA-chunk, head). Then phase 2 does cross-chunk combine (small) then output projection via W_v absorb + o_proj. Let me now also think: for the score computation, we need q_abs[h] (512) and q_rope[h] (64). Dot with c_kv[l] (512, bf16) + k_rope[l] (64). This is a GEMV per head: (L×576) @ q — the cache reads dominate at 16384: 18.9MB. Split across CTAs by position chunks: total flops = L×576×32×2 = 1.2 GFLOP at 16k — trivial compute, bandwidth ~19MB. OK. And combine: acc[h][512] weighted. Alternatively simpler "two-pass": pass1 compute scores for all L (L×32 floats = 2MB for 16k... write/read 2×2 = 4MB) then softmax, pass2 weighted sum. Extra traffic 4MB, but avoids partial state complexity. Online-softmax single pass is better: read cache once (18.9MB) vs twice (37.7MB) + scores (4MB). Definitely single-pass online softmax. Numerics: reference computes scores in fp32, softmax fp32, output in fp32 einsum, then bf16 for o_proj. Online softmax in fp32 matches well within cosine 0.98. Precision considerations throughout: reference does most math in fp32 (dequantized bf16 weights cast to float, activations float, then final .to(bf16) at linear outputs). Our kernel: load bf16/int4, dequant to fp32 (or bf16→fp32), accumulate in fp32. Should easily beat 0.98 cosine... need cos ≥ 0.98, and they give identical input state. bf16 rounding differences produce cosine like 0.9999. Fine. Now the elephant: writing this giant kernel in Triton. Complexity is high; risk of bugs. Alternative: CUDA C++ via load_inline. Also one __global__ launch. CUDA gives precise control (cp.async, ldmatrix not needed at M=1; we need wide LDG + FMA). Honestly, for int4 GEMV, CUDA might be simpler to get peak: each thread reads 16B (uint4 = 32 int4 along K... wait packing is along in (K): w_q (in//2, out), so a column n has in//2 bytes contiguous? No! w_q row-major (in//2, out): element (k2, n) at address k2*out + n — strided by out. So along K is strided; along N contiguous. GEMV y[n] = Σ_k x[k] * W[k,n]. Weight column access = strided read of bytes (stride out bytes in packed). Bad: each byte row read touches out bytes; for coalescing we want threads to read consecutive n for same k2 row: row k2 is `out` contiguous bytes covering n=0..out-1. So parallelize: block over n-chunk (say 256 columns), loop over k2 rows (in//2 rows), each thread reads consecutive bytes → coalesced rows. Per row of 256 bytes (256 cols × 1 byte = 2 int4 each), we get W[k=2*k2, n], W[k=2*k2+1, n] for 256 n's. Multiply by x[2*k2], x[2*k2+1] (broadcast from smem), dequant with scales/zeros[group(k), n]. Accumulate fp32 per column in registers; each thread owns 1-2 columns... with 256 threads and 256 cols, thread t owns col t, loops over all in//2 rows: reads 1 byte/row. Bytes in flight per thread = 1 — poor. Unroll by 8 rows: each thread issues 8 independent 1-byte loads... 1-byte loads are inefficient (32B sector per 32 threads? no wait — coalesced across threads: 256 consecutive n → 256 consecutive bytes per row-load = 8 sectors fully used per warp of 32 → fine coalescing!). Per warp per row: 32 threads × 1B = 32B = 1 sector. Utilization OK but sector-per-request efficiency: each warp request = 32B → but L2→L1 fetches 32B sectors, fine. Peak bandwidth needs many concurrent requests. Alternatively transpose the weights at load time (repack once per weight-load in Model init — allowed? We load reference weights via load_state_dict into identical buffer names, but Model.step can pre-transform once (e.g., lazily on first step) into a custom layout cached on the module. The state_dict loading must match names; but we can keep the original buffers AND precompute derived tensors (transposed/repacked) on first step or in a prepare() called lazily. That's totally fine — preprocessing at init/first-call isn't part of the timed step... well actually warmup includes first step, and benchmark warms up 3 steps before timing. Precompute in `Model.__init__`? load_state_dict happens after init, so derived tensors would be stale. So do lazy repack on first step() call (cache a "prepared" flag + a device pointer table). Repacking w_q (in//2, out) → (out, in//2) transposed: then each output column n's packed weights are contiguous (in//2 bytes, e.g., 2304/2 = 1152B). A GEMV kernel where a warp handles column n: 32 threads read consecutive bytes of that column = coalesced 1-byte loads → 32B/request... or use uint4 loads: thread reads 16B = 32 weights. in//2 = 1152B = 72 uint4s per column. Per column: 1152 bytes. Each thread processes 16B → 2-3 threads per column... With transpose, a warp could handle 32 columns with per-thread 16B strided... hmm. Honestly the cleanest high-bandwidth int4 GEMV: weight stored (N, K/2) row-major (transpose). Each CTA/warp owns a set of output columns; threads in a warp read consecutive 16B chunks along K of the SAME column — i.e., 32 threads × 16B = 512B contiguous per warp request = 4 sectors × ... = perfect coalescing (128B lines). Each thread converts 16B→32 int4s, and does 32 FMAs against x[k..k+32] from smem... Wait, but x per column is shared across all columns; the column's k-range differs per thread (thread t handles k = 32t..32t+31). So each thread needs x[32t..32t+31] — 32 floats from smem, plus dequant scale/zero for its k-group: k range 32t..32t+31 within group g = k//128. Thread t covers 32 k's = quarter of a group for group=128. Fine: 2 bf16 loads. Per output column n: acc = Σ over all k. In-warp reduction: 32 threads each hold partial acc for column n over their k-range → warp shuffle reduce (5 shuffles). Then next column. Cost: shuffles add overhead but minor vs memory. Alternatively skip warp reduce: assign each THREAD one full column (K≤2304 → per column 1152B = 72 uint4 loads sequential per thread — each load 16B, coalesced across threads only if adjacent threads read adjacent addresses — but adjacent threads own different columns → addresses differ by 1152B → NOT coalesced within a warp; but across the CTA-grid... each thread reads a distinct 1152B stream; DRAM sees many streams — bad coalescing at warp granularity: a warp's load = 32 scattered 16B = 32 sectors, but each 16B of a 32B sector → 50% over-fetch... actually 16B within a 32B sector: two adjacent threads' 16B would coalesce if same sector — not here. 50% bandwidth waste. No good.) So: warp-per-column with 16B per thread + shuffle reduce, or block-per-column-tile with cross-thread reduce via smem. Warp-per-column: per column K=2304: 72 uint4 = 72×16 = 1152B; warp iteration: each of 32 threads loads one uint4 (16B) → covers 512B → 2.25 iterations?? 1152/512 = 2.25 — not integer; pad columns to 1280B? Repack with padding to make K/2 a multiple of 512B → K=2304: 1152B pad to 1280 → 2.5 → pad to 1536B (K'=3072)? Wasteful. Alternatively warp handles k-range in chunks of 32 int4-groups... let me not over-index on exactly warp-per-column; instead: block (256 threads = 8 warps) owns TILE_N = 8 columns × ... hmm. Actually more standard: assign each warp to a contiguous k-slab of a column set: warp processes columns in tiles of N_TILE=32 (one col per lane), k-slab of 64 rows: lane l reads col n0+l, rows k0..k0+63: 32 bytes per lane = 2×16B loads, per warp per 16B-load-step: addresses n0+l... NO — with transposed layout (N, K/2), lane l reads (n0+l)*K/2 + k — addresses stride K/2 apart — again uncoalesced! Right: for coalesced 16B-per-lane loads with 32 lanes = 512B contiguous, all lanes must read consecutive 16B chunks = along K of ONE column. So warp-per-column it is (or warp iterates column-tile sequentially). With K/2 = 1152B = 72 uint4 per column: lane t processes uint4 indices t, t+32, t+64 → 3 iterations (last only 8 lanes active: 72 = 2×32 + 8 → 3rd iteration 8/32 lanes). Meh, 92% util on that load. Take K in units: 2304 → 1152B. Per warp: 3 ldg rounds (2 full + 1 partial). Shuffle reduce after column done. Actually per-column latency-bound chain: load round 1, FMA×32... the FMAs per 16B: 32 int4 → 32 FMA + 2 scale/zero loads + unpack math (shift/and ×32, cvtf... bit ops). That's a lot of ALU per byte: at 1.8TB/s with say 188×8 warps... Total unpack ALU: per int4 ~ 4-6 ops (and, shift, sub-zero, mul-scale, fma, cvt). Weights 213MB = 426M int4 → ~2.5G ops ALU — GPU has ~ 188 SM × 128 lanes × 2GHz × 2 (two ops?) ≈ 100 TOPS → 25µs. OK fine. But unpack can use byte-level tricks: read 16B as 4×uint32; each uint32 = 8 int4s (along K! since packing along K: byte k2 = (W[2k2] , W[2k2+1]) nibbles... wait transposed layout: for column n, we stored the original column k=0..K-1 packed two per byte: byte b = W[2b] | W[2b+1]<<4. uint32 at bytes 4b..4b+3 = W[8b..8b+7]. Extract 8 nibbles → (w - z)*s — groups of 8 within same 128-group → same s/z for 16 consecutive uint32s. Use LOP3-style tricks or PRMT? Simple approach fine. Now in Triton, can we get this level? Triton auto-vectorizes contiguous loads: if we do tl.load of a (BLOCK_K,) block with proper layout, it emits wide loads. Writing warp-per-column + shuffle reduction in Triton: awkward. In Triton, natural formulation: block computes a (BLOCK_N,) tile of outputs; load W tile (BLOCK_K_BYTES, BLOCK_N) ... with transposed layout (N, K/2): load (BLOCK_N, BLOCK_KB) tile → reduce over KB. Triton handles reduction over axis with efficient codegen. Loads: rows along N are strided; each row contiguous KB bytes — Triton will vectorize along the last dim and coalesce across threads... generally fine. I think Triton CAN express everything, but the whole-megakernel-in-Triton with all phases is going to be one gigantic function; risk management: implement phase helpers as @triton.jit functions called from the main kernel (inlined). That's fine. Alternative: CUDA C++ load_inline with phases as device functions + cooperative-style barrier (grid.sync via our own barrier to avoid cooperative launch dependency... cooperative launch requires cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel — via load_inline we can call it, but simpler custom barrier). CUDA gives us full control for bandwidth. But code complexity also high. Let me consider effort: Triton version — I know how to write fused int4 GEMV in Triton at near-peak: patterns from e.g. GPTQ Triton kernels. The barrier trick in Triton is proven (e.g., persistent kernels / "stream-k" use atomics; spin barriers used in several Triton megakernels like the "one-shot allreduce" kernels). Actually — hmm, wait. Let me reconsider the approach entirely. Is there a fundamentally simpler decomposition? What about ONE CTA doing sequential dependent tiny phases while other CTAs do the bandwidth-heavy streaming... The dependencies per block are hard serialization points (rmsnorm needs full vector). No way around barriers. Let me count the ACTUAL minimum bytes and estimate times per phase to see budget: Per KDA block: weights 23.6MB int4 + scales/zeros (~2×2B×(4×18×4096 + 32×2304)... ≈ 1.1MB) ≈ 24.7MB. Conv/state small. S state: read+write 2MB per layer (bf16). MoE: 9 experts × 3.54MB = 31.9MB + scales ~1MB. Per KDA block total ≈ 58MB → ×3 = 173MB. MLA block: q_proj 7.08 + kv_a 0.66 + kv_b 2.11 (+re-read 2.11 if twice) + o_proj 4.72 ≈ 14.6MB + cache 2.4-18.9MB + MoE 33MB ≈ 50-67MB. Total ≈ 223-240MB → at 1.7TB/s effective ≈ 131-141µs → ~7000 tok/s. Baseline est ~2.9GB+ overheads → ≥1.6ms. Speedup ≥ 10×. Time to check environment: GPU details, Triton version. Let me run nvidia-smi and check torch/triton. Plan of attack: 1. Inspect environment (GPU, torch, triton versions). 2. Run reference + baseline to get baseline latency numbers. 3. Implement solution.py: - Model class with same parameters/buffers (load reference state_dict). - Lazy "prepare" step: repack all weights into fused-friendly flat buffers; precompute pointer table... For Triton, pass many pointers as kernel args (max ~ limited but plenty: we have ~4 blocks × (q,k,v,g,o w/s/z + conv + beta + router + expert tensors) — since experts are single big tensors (64,...), per block we need ~20 pointers × 4 blocks + scratch ~10 → ~90 args. Triton supports many args (up to some limit, ~256 fine). Alternatively pack pointers into a device int64 tensor and index in-kernel (adds a load per pointer — fine, or even better: transpile all weights into ONE flat weight blob per block and use offsets computed in-kernel (constexpr) — since all shapes static, precompute byte offsets on host, pass base pointer + few offsets as constexpr). I'll flatten everything into one contiguous buffer per block-type, or even one big blob total, offsets as Python ints → constexpr in kernel. Clean! 4. Write the megakernel with phases + barriers. 5. check.py until PASS, benchmark, profile, iterate. Fallback if full megakernel too buggy: still must be single launch; could do simpler-but-slower in-kernel phases. Note steps: benchmark runs multiple decode steps (32/16/16 steps) — each step = one launch; state persists across launches on device (S, conv windows, cache). Between steps we can even keep scratch. Barrier slots need the monotonic target trick — pass `bar_base` (cumulative expected count) as arg per launch, bump by grid each call. Since grid fixed. Wait, actually also we could make step() do a launch that processes... no — one step = one token = one launch. The timed steps each do one launch. Good. Correctness details to mirror EXACTLY (cosine 0.98 only, but states compared too): - KDA S update with g = -softplus(g_proj(x))/(...) then exp; beta = sigmoid; S = S*exp(g); pred = Σ_dk S*k; S += beta * k⊗(v-pred); o = Σ_dk S*q. - Note S layout: st["S"] (H, Dk, Dv) — but is our in-kernel S the same buffer? State dict comes from reference.init_state each step (fresh dict with tensors); we must update st["S"] in place (check compares st_sol after step). Our kernel writes into the same tensors (we pass pointers). Note init_state creates tensors on cuda; the same st dict is reused across steps in benchmark via returned state. In reference, st["S"] = S reassignment replaces tensor. In our impl: write in place, return same dict. - MLA: append c_kv (512) and k_rope (64) to cache via torch.cat — reference CATs creating new tensor each step → state dict gets new tensor. Our kernel must handle growing L across steps: we can either (a) also cat outside kernel (extra kernels — not allowed; cat launches a kernel!) — hmm. torch.cat of (L,512)+(1,512) launches a copy kernel = extra launch, forbidden ("no Python loop that fires a series of small kernels per token"; one launch total). Solution: maintain our own preallocated cache buffer (max_len) inside state dict? State dict from reference.init_state has exact tensors st["c_kv"]; check compares st_sol["c_kv"] AFTER one step vs reference's cat result (L+1). If we replace st["c_kv"] with our own tensor (a slice view of a bigger buffer?), cosine sim flatten works on views fine. But benchmark steps chain: step s uses returned state (with L+1). We can return NEW tensors that are views into a preallocated (ctx+steps+pad, 512) buffer. First step: copy init c_kv into our buffer? That's an extra kernel (copy) on the FIRST step only — but first steps are warmup (3 warmup steps! _run with min(3,steps) — warmup includes step 1). Hmm but "the decode step that check.py and benchmark.py time must be a SINGLE GPU kernel launch" — check.py calls step once per seed/ctx (not warmup). If our first-call-per-state does an extra copy kernel, check.py's single step would use 2 launches... Risky for the authenticity judge? The judge reads code; a one-time "materialize cache into workspace" on a fresh state could be framed as state preparation, but it IS an extra kernel launch inside step, every step (since check creates fresh state each time → always first step for that state!). Bad. Better: pass the reference cache tensors directly to the kernel on first use (L×512 at some pointer) and write the appended token... but torch.cat in reference semantics: st["c_kv"] must BECOME a (L+1, 512) tensor. We can't append in place (tensor is exactly L). We must create a new tensor and copy L rows — memcpy of 16MB at 16k = ~9µs, plus it's another kernel launch (d2d copy kernel). That breaks single-launch. Solution: allocate our own persistent cache workspace ONCE (per MLA layer, max size ctx + 64 steps... but ctx varies per check; reallocate if needed — allocation isn't a kernel) and make st["c_kv"] a VIEW (slice) of it. But the initial L rows must be copied from reference's tensor — a copy kernel... UNLESS the megakernel itself does it: on step call, kernel reads c_kv from BOTH... no — attention needs the whole cache; kernel can read from wherever. But st must END as a (L+1) valid tensor for the checker. If st["c_kv"] becomes view of our workspace, the kernel must write rows 0..L (copy old cache) + row L (new). Copying L×576×2 bytes = 18.9MB inside the kernel + attention reading 18.9MB → extra 37MB write+read... adds ~20-40µs. Ugly but works and stays single-launch. Alternative cleaner: track generation. On the FIRST step with a given state (detect via a `st["_sol_meta"]` marker key? we can't add keys? We CAN add keys to the state dict — check only reads st[mla_layer]["c_kv"] etc. Adding keys like st["_gen"] is safe... wait check does `st_sol[kda_layer]["S"]` — our own keys are ignored. And benchmark passes state back; fine.) So: on first step for a state, kernel reads cache directly from reference tensors (pass their pointers, length L), computes new token's c_kv/k_rope, writes into our workspace buffer rows L, then we CREATE view tensors c_kv_new = ws_c[:L+1] — BUT the old L rows in the workspace are garbage (we never copied!). The view would contain garbage in rows 0..L-1. check.py flattens and cosines the WHOLE (L+1)×512 → garbage → FAIL. So rows 0..L-1 must contain correct values in whatever st["c_kv"] ends up pointing to. Unless st["c_kv"] ends as the ORIGINAL ref tensor plus... no, must be L+1 rows. Option: make the kernel write new token values into a fresh (L+1,576) tensor: allocate new tensor of (L+1, 512) via torch.empty (allocation, no kernel!), then kernel writes... the first L rows still need copying from old tensor. Copy = kernel. UNLESS the megakernel does the copy: read old cache (18.9MB), write to new tensor (18.9MB), while ALSO doing attention on it. Extra ~19MB traffic (+10µs at 16k). And attention reads from old tensor fine. Alternatively: maintain workspace + copy lazily but do the copy INSIDE the megakernel on first step only (horrid branchy). Hmm wait — actually can we avoid the copy by having st["c_kv"] be a tensor SHARE with the reference-produced values...? What if we make our own state the "source of truth" from step 1: on the very first step, st["c_kv"] input is the reference tensor (L rows). Attention reads it directly (no copy). We allocate ws (Lmax, 512). Kernel writes new token c_kv into ws row L. Then set st["c_kv"] = torch.cat? No. Trick: st_new["c_kv"] = ws[:L+1] where we COPY rows 0..L-1 — the copy is unavoidable if the final tensor must be a fresh contiguous thing... UNLESS the checker only cosines... no it cosines the whole thing. OK accepting: inside the megakernel, phase: copy old cache into workspace/new tensor (parallel trivial), compute+append new row, attention uses workspace after barrier (or old tensor directly — same values). Extra traffic: write L×576×2 (18.9MB at 16k). Total budget ~260MB → 145µs. Acceptable-ish. BUT WAIT — actually can we be smarter: allocate ws ONCE per (model, max ctx 16384+steps). st = reference.init_state → c_kv tensor A (L rows). First step: kernel: (i) attention reads A; (ii) copies A into ws rows 0..L-1 AND writes new row L; st["c_kv"]=ws[:L+1]. Second step: st["c_kv"] IS ws[:L+1] (detect via data_ptr or marker key) → attention reads ws directly, appends at row L+1 → st["c_kv"]=ws[:L+2]. No copies after first step. The first step pays the copy but that's the warmup step in benchmark... but check.py measures nothing, just correctness — fine! And chained steps only re-copy if a fresh state arrives (check uses fresh state each seed/ctx — one step each — so each check step pays copy once: +19MB ≈ fine). Detection: does st["c_kv"].data_ptr() == ws.data_ptr()? Use marker: store on the state dict `st["_ws"] = (ws_c, ws_k, L)`. If marker exists and marker's ws is our buffer → fast path. Marker keys start with "_" — check.py accesses st_sol[mla_layer]["c_kv"] and st_ref[...] — no interference. Reference model gets st_ref — untouched. Also KDA state S: reference step reassigns st["S"] = S (in-place实际上 it's `st["S"] = S` rebinding — but S is mutated in place earlier: `S = st["S"] * ...` creates NEW tensor; then st["S"] = S. So reference returns new tensors for S. Our kernel writes S in place into st["S"]'s memory — semantically equivalent for check (they compare st_sol["S"] values). Continuous chaining fine (in-place update). Conv windows: reference st["cq"] = win[1:] → new tensor (view). We can update in place with a shift... in-kernel: read window + write updated window (overlap within same buffer; kernel reads all then writes — fine if same thread reads-then-writes its chunk; do via registers). Store as (3, C) window in our workspace; on input st["cq"] shape (3, C) — same layout. In-place shift OK. Return same tensors. Hmm wait, one more subtlety: check.py compares `st_ref[kda]["S"]` vs `st_sol[kda]["S"]` — reference's st_ref["S"] is a NEW tensor (fp32? `S = st["S"] * g.exp()` → randn fp32? init_state S is fp32 (torch.randn default fp32, no dtype) — yes S is float32 in init_state and stays fp32). Ours updates in place fp32. Good: keep S fp32 in-kernel. Conv windows cq/ck/cv are bf16 (created with dtype cfg.dtype). Kernel reads 3×C bf16, computes, writes shifted window bf16 (win[1:] = rows 1,2 + new) — represent as buffer (3,C): new row0=old row1, row1=old row2, row2=val(new pre-conv activation? win = cat([prev, val]) → window stores RAW val (pre-conv), yes q_proj outputs. Write back bf16: reference cq is bf16 and stored vals are q_proj outputs bf16 from reference... in reference, q = self.q_proj(x) → bf16 (QuantLinear returns bf16); win bf16. Round to bf16 on store matches. MLA cache values: c_kv bf16 from kv_a output (bf16); k_rope: bf16 after rope (rope applied then cat: `_apply_rope(k_rope, cos, sin)` returns x.dtype = bf16). store bf16. In check, cos sim over ref cache (bf16 values from their computation) vs ours (bf16 of our computation) — fp32 internal math then bf16 round — diffs ~1e-3 relative — cosine ~0.99999 fine. Now the MoE router tie-break: torch.topk on 64 probs — deterministic; our kernel computes fp32 probs identically? Reference: router weight bf16, x bf16 → matmul in bf16?? baseline: `torch.softmax(self.router(x).float(), -1)` — router(x) is bf16 GEMV (PyTorch bf16 matmul, fp32 accum, bf16 out). fp32 exp/normalize. topk picks 8 indices; ties extremely unlikely. Our kernel: fp32 GEMV on bf16 inputs → tiny numeric diffs in logits. Top-8 set could differ on near-ties... if top8 differs from reference → output differs substantially → cosine drop below 0.98?? A differing 8th expert (weight w8 ~ small) — rerouting one expert of 8 slightly changes output; cosine probably still > 0.99? The routed weights are softmaxed & renormalized; expert outputs are O(expert FFN) — replacing one expert changes out by ~ w_e*(ffn_e - ffn_alt) — with scaling 2.446... risky but near-ties are the only failure case and they swap near-identical probs. If probs are nearly equal, swapping still changes output by difference of expert FFNs weighted — not negligible. BUT probability of near-tie with random init is low across 3 seeds × 2 ctx × 4 blocks... eh, still — to be safer: match reference numerics precisely: compute router logits as bf16-rounded result of fp32-accumulated GEMV over bf16 inputs, THEN softmax in fp32. PyTorch bf16 matmul: accumulates fp32, rounds output to bf16. We can replicate exactly (same accumulation order? no — order differs → tiny diffs in bf16 rounding → possible flip on knife-edge ties only; measure: probability of two of 64 random logits within 1e-3? logits std ~ sqrt(2304)*0.02*0.25 ≈ 0.24; softmax probs ~ 0.01-0.03; near-tie within bf16 quantum (~2e-3 relative) at top-8/9 boundary: probability maybe ~1% per decision... over 4 blocks × 6 trials = 24 decisions → ~20% one flip. Hmm worry. But wait — ALSO baseline.py has the same exposure (benchmark compares solution vs REFERENCE directly, not vs baseline — so only OUR flips matter). Reference router: `self.router(x)` where x is `_rmsnorm(h,...)` bf16... fp32? x bf16, weight bf16 → PyTorch bf16 matmul (fp32 acc). Output bf16 → .float() → softmax → topk. Can we make our kernel's router GEMV bit-exact to PyTorch's? PyTorch bf16 GEMV internally: torch.mm on bf16 → cublas with fp32 accumulate, but tiling order unknown → same accumulation order as our kernel unlikely. Bit-exactness is unattainable; rely on tie probability being low + cosine slack: even IF the 8th expert flips, how much does cosine move? Output after rerouting: difference vector d = w8*ffn(e8) - w8'*ffn(e8') likely magnitude ~ (0.05..0.1 of routed weight sum)×O(1)·... vs total output norm sqrt(2304)*scale... I'd estimate cosine stays ≥ 0.99 in most flips. Actually a flipped expert has prob ≈ the one it replaced; the weighted renormalization w = w/sum*2.446 — similar. The FFN outputs for different random experts are near-orthogonal: |d| ~ w * |ffn| ~ w * (sqrt(2304)*0.02*... ffn output element ~ N(0, sqrt(1024)*(0.02^2)*...) hmm elements ~ 0.02*0.02*sqrt(2*2304/2)... whatever — |ffn| ≈ 2.5 (element std ~ 0.005, ×sqrt(2304)≈48*0.005=0.24?) Let me not spiral: |d|/|out| maybe 0.02-0.1 → cosine ≥ 0.995. Fine, accept the risk; mitigate by matching math order closely (fp32 accumulate in same k-order — sequential over k! Our per-column accumulation over k in natural order ≈ torch's split-k order? torch doesn't split-k for GEMV usually... cublas GEMV bf16... close enough). Actually hmm, wait. Let me reconsider: does solution get compared to reference with THE SAME topk logic such that flips even matter? Yes — reference picks its top-8, we pick ours; if different experts, outputs differ by one expert's FFN × its weight. Analysis above says cosine ≈ 0.99+. Accept. Top-k implementation in-kernel: 64 logits → each CTA computes all 64 logits redundantly (64×2304 bf16 weight reads = 288KB from L2 per CTA — hmm ×188 CTAs × 4 blocks — wait from L2! 288KB × 188 = 54MB of L2 traffic per block, at ~10TB/s L2... 5µs. per block. 4 blocks → hmm 20µs total. That's too much? L2 BW on Blackwell ~ 10+TB/s → 54MB / 10TB/s = 5.4µs per MoE → 21µs total ≈ 15% overhead. Alternative: compute router logits ONCE in a prior phase by one warp, write 64 floats to scratch; all CTAs read 256B + do identical topk → L2 traffic trivial. Fold router computation into... it needs full rmsnorm(h) → earliest = first MoE phase, but expert selection needed in THAT phase (gate/up GEMV). So: MoE phase 1a: CTA0 warp0 computes logits (+softmax+topk) writes scratch+flag; other CTAs wait on flag (short spin — they're big; better: give them the shared-expert gate/up work first unconditionally, then spin for routed ids). Micro-optimization; start simple (CTA0 computes, others spin on flag with nanosleep, then proceed). Since gate/up of 9 experts is ~7µs of work, spin-on-flag costs nearly nothing if CTA0 finishes logits in ~2µs. And other CTAs can start with shared expert (id 64 known statically!) while CTA0 does router. Even simpler alternative: dedicate the router to the tail of the o_proj phase: after computing o_proj outputs + residual h (needs ALL h for rmsnorm) — no. Router input = rmsnorm(h) needs full h. So no earlier than after barrier following o_proj phase. OK. Wait, actually, I realize we could also COMPUTE rmsnorm redundantly per CTA cheaply since h is only 2304: each CTA reads h (4.6KB) computes sumsq → norm — that's 4.6KB per CTA per norm — trivial. So every CTA can have normed x locally at phase start! Only router needs the spin trick (or... every CTA also computes router redundantly = 288KB L2 reads per CTA... no. Use CTA0 warp + flag.) Hmm, actually reconsider: could ALL CTAs read the SAME 64×2304 bf16 router weight — every CTA reads the same 288KB; after the first CTA pulls it into L2, others hit L2. 288KB × 188 CTAs = 54MB L2 → yeah as computed. L2 BW maybe 15-20TB/s on Blackwell → ~3µs per block, 12µs total, ~8%. Try CTA0+flag first. Now the biggest challenge remains writing phase code for each part in Triton. Let me reconsider CUDA C++: full control, standard patterns (shuffle reduce, cp.async optional). A single big __global__ with switch on phase, grid-stride loops. Debug via printf. I lean CUDA C++ for the megakernel correctness-critical parts: Phase code sketch (CUDA): - int4 GEMV fused: transposed layout (N, K/2) pre-repacked on host in prepare (lazily at first step — but repack uses torch ops → kernels! prepare happens INSIDE first step()? No — do prepare lazily at first step but it's one-time per weight-set; check.py calls step multiple seeds → same model, weights unchanged → prepare once. Extra kernels during prepare = launch before the megakernel in that step... happens on EVERY check scenario? Each check seed creates fresh state but SAME model → prepare cached on model → only first step total pays. check.py: creates sol_model, loads weights, then seed 0 ctx 2048 → step 1 → prepare (several kernels) + megakernel. Is that a megakernel violation? The judge cares that the TIMED path is one kernel; prepare-once is standard (weight repacking). benchmark.py warms up 3 steps before timing → prepare lands in warmup. I think acceptable and honest: repacking must happen sometime. Alternatively prepare in... load_state_dict is a plain nn.Module load; we can hook `_load_from_state_dict`? Simpler: override load_state_dict wrapper: call super then prepare. check calls sol_model.load_state_dict(ref state) — hooking is legit, keep it simple: lazy flag checked in step; prepare idempotent; also expose explicit `prepare()` called at end of load_state_dict if we override it. I'll override load_state_dict to trigger prepare after loading. Then step() only ever launches the megakernel. Wait, is repack even needed? Reading (in/2, out) rows directly: coalescing across n (out) per row k2: fine as analyzed (row-major reads). Per phase GEMV tile: BLOCK_N columns × sequential k2 rows. Each row read = BLOCK_N bytes contiguous (need out ≥ BLOCK_N... out sizes 4096, 2304, 1024, 6144, 576, 8192 — choose BLOCK_N=256/512). Bytes per thread per row: BLOCK_N/256 threads = 1-2B. Then k-loop rows: per warp: consecutive threads read consecutive bytes — a warp reads 32 consecutive bytes (BLOCK_N ≥ 32 across threads... with 256 threads and BLOCK_N=256: thread t ↔ byte t: warp = 32B contiguous = 1 sector/request; 4 sectors per warp... whatever — requests are 32B, utilization 100% (all bytes used). DRAM efficiency OK but request rate: 1.8TB/s / 32B = 56G requests/s vs GPU request throughput ~ 188 SM × 4 LSU × 2GHz... fine. BUT the x[k] multiply: thread t reading (k2, n=t) gets W[2k2,n], W[2k2+1,n] → needs x[2k2], x[2k2+1] broadcast — same for all threads in block → from smem. Per byte: 2 FMA + dequant ops + scale/zero access per group (k2//64 since group=128 → 64 packed rows per group). scale/zero (in//128, out): per 64 rows, all threads read s[g, n=t], z[g, n=t] — consecutive t → coalesced bf16 loads ×2. Accumulators: 1 float per thread (its column). in=2304 → 1152 rows. Per thread: 1152 iterations × (1B load + 2FMA + ~6 ALU). Latency per iter ~ L2/DRAM latency hidden by unrolling (independent loads) — Triton/CUDA can pipeline; with 188×256 threads × ~8 independent loads in flight → 385KB in flight. Might not fully saturate 1.8TB/s. Increase per-thread work: thread handles 2-4 columns (BLOCK_N = 512-1024 with 256 threads): more independent loads/thread, less x reload, accumulators ×4. With transpose repack: (N, K/2) rows of K/2 bytes each. Block of 256 threads processes one column n at a time? or... thread t reads bytes t*?? to coalesce 16B/lane: assign lane l bytes [16l, 16l+16) of column n: warp covers 512B contiguous — perfect. Warp loops: column n, k-chunk = 512B (1024 int4 = K of 1024): lanes hold 16B each. Per 16B: 32 weights → 32 FMA with x[k0+32l .. +32] from smem + one (s,z) per 128-k group (16B spans k=32l..32l+31 → group k//128 = l//4). Reduction: after full K (1152B = 2.25 chunks — again ragged for K=2304; pad repack rows to 1280B or make lanes handle variable bytes: 1152/16 = 72 → lanes 0..7 do 3 chunks... loop ceil(72/32)=3 with tail masking (lane's 3rd chunk only if 32+lane < 72 → lanes 0..7). Fine, masked). Then warp-shuffle reduce 5 steps → lane0 writes y[n]. Warp iterates over next column n += num_warps... Which is better? Transposed: 16B/lane coalesced, in-warp reduce overhead (5 shuffles + broadcast per column per warp: per column 1152B → 36B/lane shuffle cost trivial). x smem reads: each lane reads 32 floats per 16B chunk from smem — 128B/lane per 16B... x smem traffic = 32× lane bytes — smem BW fine. Also each lane needs s/z (bf16 pairs) per 4 lanes×?? whatever. Per-block both fine. The transpose repack makes the kernel cleaner and faster; do it in prepare (torch ops on GPU — permute+contiguous+pad; allowed since it's our own preprocessing, not a "prebuilt quant kernel"). Hmm, wait — do I even need transpose? Consider simplicity: packed (K/2, N) with thread-per-column: the loop is fully sequential per thread over 1152 rows — each thread has ~8 outstanding loads with unroll-8 → 8B in flight per thread × 48K threads = 385KB. Short of the ~1.4MB needed. Unroll 32 → 1.5MB. That's a LOT of unroll for dependency chains (acc FMA serializes but loads independent). Registers: fine (acc + few). Actually with thread-per-column the FMA chain: acc += ... serial dependency per column of 2304 FMAs = latency chain 2304×4cy=9216 cycles vs loads... it's throughput bound overall, FMA chain fine (interleave 2-4 columns per thread to break chains). I think I'll do the PREPACK to a per-thread friendly layout. Actually, you know what — let me look at what GPTQ Triton kernels do: they keep (K/2, N) packed uint8 loaded as (BLOCK_K/2? ...) with int32 loads (BLOCK_K=32 nibbles in 4 uint32? they load uint8 tensor as int32 via bitcast? In Triton you can load uint8 tensor with shape (K/2, N) blocks (BK2, BN); unpack in registers; multiply. Triton generates vectorized loads when BN contiguous and threads assigned along N. Reduction along K via tl.sum. It's clean in Triton: ``` offs_k2 = pid_k*BK2 + arange(BK2) # packed rows offs_n = pid_n*BN + arange(BN) w = tl.load(w_ptr + offs_k2[:,None]*N + offs_n[None,:]) # (BK2, BN) uint8 lo = (w & 0xF).to(float32); hi = (w >> 4).to(float32) # scales: group = (2*offs_k2)//128 s = tl.load(scales + g[:,None]*N + offs_n[None,:]) x_lo = tl.load(x_ptr + 2*offs_k2) # hmm x fp32 in smem anyway acc += tl.sum(lo * x_lo[:,None] * s ...) ``` This is a standard fused dequant GEMM tile. Triton handles vectorization (uint8 loads (BK2,BN): each row BN contiguous → LDG 32-bit+ per thread if BN/threads ≥4). Set BN=128/256, threads along N: 256 threads → 128 cols → 2 cols... Triton figures it out. But this is per-GEMV-tile Triton style. For the MEGAKERNEL all phases live in one function; phases = code blocks with barriers between, work distributed via `pid` over flattened work items. Fine. Decision: TRITON. Reasons: faster iteration than raw CUDA; tl.* handles vectorization; spin barriers doable; one @triton.jit launch is explicitly allowed ("one @triton.jit grid launch"). Also phases benefit from Triton's block abstractions for the conv/S-update/attention math. Downside risk: Triton spin-loop support — `while` cond on scalar works. Atomic add with sem works (tl.atomic_add(ptr, val, sem="acq_rel", scope="gpu") — default scope gpu). volatile load: tl.load(ptr, volatile=True) exists. One more design question: the kernel needs MANY pointers. Rather than 100+ args, pass a single int64 "pointer table" device buffer? Triton kernels can take tensor args (pointer+... only data pointer passed). Loading a pointer from memory then using it as pointer — Triton can't cast int64→pointer easily... Actually tl.cast(int64, tl.pointer_type(...))? There's `tl.inline_asm_elementwise` for raw... Triton does support int-to-pointer via `tl.cast(x, tl.pi32_t)`? Not reliably. Better: pass each tensor as a separate kernel argument (Triton supports many; ~100 args is fine — launches via cached cubin have some arg overhead but we launch ONCE). Since shapes are static, bake all strides/offsets as constexpr where possible. Alternatively flatten all weights into ONE big uint8 buffer + one scales buffer + one zeros buffer with static offsets per weight (computed in Python). Then kernel args: wq_ptr, s_ptr, z_ptr + a few. Scales/zeros are bf16 tensors possibly different shapes per weight — flatten both into one bf16 buffer each. Non-quant weights (conv_w, beta_w, router_w, norms): flatten into one bf16 "aux" buffer. Scratch: one big f32/byte buffer with offsets. State tensors (S, conv windows, caches) are external pointers (they're in the state dict — can't be flattened; but we CAN copy them into our persistent workspace during... copying state into workspace each step = extra kernel + traffic. Better: pass the small number of state pointers directly: 3 KDA layers × (S, cq, ck, cv) = 12 ptrs + MLA cache ptrs (c_kv, k_rope) ×2 + lengths. S: 32×128×128 fp32 = 2MB/layer — fine as-is. Wait, but there's a subtlety for MLA workspace: as designed, on non-first steps st["c_kv"] is a view into OUR workspace; kernel just needs the two pointers + L. We pass data_ptr of st["c_kv"], st["k_rope"] directly each call. On first step for fresh state: kernel (i) reads cache from given ptr, (ii) also copies it to workspace, (iii) st dict updated to views. But then NEXT step the st["c_kv"] view IS workspace[:L+1] → data_ptr same → skip copy. Detect "need_copy" host-side: st.get("_ws") marker. Kernel arg: copy_dst ptr (or nullptr → skip; Triton can't do nullptr branches well... pass copy flag int + dst ptr). Hmm, actually — wait. Simpler management: ALWAYS maintain our own cache workspace and make the kernel ALWAYS do the copy when given a "fresh" external cache (flag), and SKIP when state is already ours (marker). The copy phase: parallel trivial: L×(512+64) bf16 copy = 2.3-18.9MB r+w. Add phase at start of MLA block (before attention barrier). OK. Actually hmm, even simpler: since reference.init_state returns a dict and we fully control st dict for OUR model, on FIRST step replace st["c_kv"] with workspace views and stash originals? The kernel still must copy rows 0..L-1 once. Yes as said. Total extra ~10-20µs on first step per fresh state; benchmark warms up 3 steps → first step of the whole run absorbs it... wait no! _time does `_run(...min(3,steps))` warmup ONCE per (model,ctx), then times `_run(steps)` — warmup runs steps 1-3 (copy at step1), timed run starts fresh?? `_time` calls `_run` which calls `ref.init_state` FRESH each call!! Look: `_run(model, cfg, ctx, steps, seed, ref): st = ref.init_state(...)` — fresh state EVERY _run call, including timed ones! So the FIRST step of every timed run pays the copy (and L grows 16k→16k+16 over 16 steps). So timed latency includes one copy of the cache per run — amortized over steps (16-32). Copy once 18.9MB w + attention must read it anyway → +36MB on step1 ≈ +20µs once per ~2.3ms run ≈ +1%. Fine. And actually during timed steps only first step is "fresh"; subsequent steps hit fast path. Wait, actually also MLA attention each step RE-reads the whole cache (18.9MB@16k) — that's inherent (16 steps × 19MB = 304MB vs weights 16×220 = 3.5GB — 8% overhead... hmm at 16k, cache read 18.9MB ×2 (score pass AND value pass? online softmax reads c_kv once, using it for scores; the weighted sum ALSO needs c_kv! acc[h,j] += p_l * c_kv[l,j] — same pass reuses loaded c_kv values in registers? In online softmax, when processing position l: compute score s_l, update m, rescale, acc += exp(s_l - m)*c_kv[l] — YES single pass reuse in registers. Total cache traffic = L×576×2 = 18.9MB once. Nice — that's the beauty of latent-space attention: 18.9MB vs fp16-sequence 537MB.) Alright — also the shared expert ids are static: expert 64 conceptually but stored in separate tensors s_gate etc. Work items: experts 0..7 (routed) + shared. Let me now also decide the KDA o = S·q: q already scaled by Dk^-0.5 — reference multiplies q by scale BEFORE conv?? Order: q = q_proj → conv+silu → then `q.view(H,Dk).float() * self.scale`. Yes scale after conv and BEFORE state use. g = -softplus(g_proj out) — note g_proj input is x (the SAME normed x). beta_proj likewise (nn.Linear bf16, sigmoid). Order of residual: h = x + attn(rmsnorm(x)); x2 = h + moe(rmsnorm(h)). RMSNorm: xf * rsqrt(mean(xf²)+eps) * w. MoE weighting: w normalized by sum+1e-9 then ×2.446; out = Σ_routed w_j*ffn + Σ_shared ffn (shared NOT scaled by weight, not normalized... shared expert output added unweighted). And note _ffn: silu(x@G)*(x@U) then @D, all fp32, output added to fp32 accum, final .to(bf16). MLA scale: (nq = 128+64=192)^-0.5 on total scores. scores fp32, softmax fp32 (dim over l), p stays fp32; o einsum in fp32 → .to(bf16) → o_proj bf16?? reference: o.reshape(...).to(torch.bfloat16) then o_proj (QuantLinear: float() @ float()). So o cast to bf16 then matmul fp32. Fine. MLA absorb changes order of operations vs reference: reference materializes k_nope[l,h,:] = c_kv@W (fp32: c_kv float @ weight float... wait baseline: st["c_kv"] bf16 @ _wbf(kv_b) bf16 → bf16 output! Hmm baseline differs from reference here. REFERENCE: `kvb = self.kv_b(st["c_kv"])...float()` — QuantLinear.forward casts to float and computes fp32 matmul, output .to(bf16)! QuantLinear.forward returns bf16. So kvb is bf16 in BOTH ref and... wait ref: `kvb = self.kv_b(st["c_kv"]).view(-1, H, ...).float()` → kvb bf16→float for the einsums (scores einsum in fp32 on bf16-rounded kvb). Our absorb: q_abs = q_nope @ W_k fp32 (no bf16 rounding of intermediate!), then scores = q_abs·c + ... Different rounding point — reference rounds kvb to bf16; we skip that but use exact fp32 product. Cosine impact: tiny (bf16 rounding ~0.4% per element, scores aggregate 128 dims → relative error ~0.4%/sqrt(128)... fine, way above 0.98). q_nope: reference q = q_proj(x) bf16 → view → q_nope .float(). We keep fp32 from kernel dequant; round not needed. scores = (einsum fp32) * scale; softmax fp32. p fp32 × v fp32→ o fp32 → bf16 → o_proj. Our: ctx[h,512] fp32 → o[h,:] = ctx@W_v fp32 → bf16 → o_proj GEMV fp32 accumulate → bf16 out. All good. RMSNorm eps 1e-6, mean over 2304 in fp32. RoPE: theta 10000, dim 64, pairs (even, odd): out[2i] = e*cos - o*sin; out[2i+1] = o*cos + e*sin. inv = theta^(-2i/64). pos = L (current cache length before append = position of new token). Precomputed? pos varies per step; compute in-kernel: lane computes cos/sin via libdevice (tl.cos/tl.sin or __nv_cosf... Triton tl.cos exists). Fine (64 angles, trivial). KDA conv: win (4, C) = [prev(3), val]; w = conv_w[idx] (C,4) transposed: conv_w is (3, H*Dk, 4) → conv_w[idx] is (C, 4) "(input_dim..., kernel)": out[c] = Σ_j win[j,c]*w[c,j] then silu. Note `.float()` ops in fp32; val bf16. Store new window (rows 1,2, val) bf16. S update (in fp32): S[h] *= exp(g[h])[:,None] (g per dk), pred[dv] = Σ_dk S[dk,dv]*k[dk], S += beta*k[dk]*(v[dv]-pred[dv]) outer... S[dk,dv] += beta * k[dk] * (v[dv]-pred[dv]), o[dv] = Σ_dk S[dk,dv]*q[dk]. All per head independently. Parallel: (h, dv-chunk). Loads k[h,:],g,q (128 each) broadcast per head; each dv lane: iterate dk=0..127: S row ops. Compute cost: 128×128×~6 flops ×32 heads×3 layers = 9.4 MFLOP — trivial. Memory: S fp32 read+write 4MB/page-layer (bf16? keep fp32: 32×16K×4 = 2MB read + 2MB write per layer → 12MB total) ≈ 6µs. OK. exp(g): 4096 exps. Wait, precision: reference stores st["S"] = S fp32 (in fp32 tensor). We write fp32 back to the SAME tensor (in-place). Numerically fp32 ops — deterministic small diffs — cosine on S vs ref: both fp32 → ~1e-6 diffs. Alright. Barrier count per step: per block: ~5; ×4 = 20. Plus a couple global ones (cache copy, final write?) The kernel writes hidden out at the end (2304 bf16 → the returned hidden tensor). Actually hidden must be a NEW tensor each step? reference returns h (new tensors). We can maintain two persistent hidden buffers and alternate; return the one written this step. It's a tensor we own; check does o_sol from returned value — fine. And hidden dtype bf16 shape (2304,). Numbers of work-items per phase (grid-stride): GEMV phases: total output columns: KDA: 4×4096 = 16384 (+option to also do... kv stuff). Tiles of BN=256 → 64 tiles ÷ 188 CTAs: CTA gets ≤1 tile. o_proj: 2304 cols / 256 = 9 tiles only → underutilization! o_proj GEMV: K=4096 (4.7MB weights) with only 9-18 tiles → 188 CTAs idle-wait at barrier → o_proj phase BW = 18/188... BAD. Need k-split: split K into chunks with fp32 atomicAdd into output (fp32 atomics fine, deterministic? atomics order varies → nondeterministic tiny fp32 diffs; fine for cosine). OR split-output finer: BN=64 → 36 tiles, still meh. Use split-K × split-N: for o_proj (K=4096, N=2304): make tiles (K-chunk 512 × N 64) = 8×36 = 288 partial-GEMV tiles, atomicAdd. Add per-tile dequant work. Scratch zeroing needed before atomics — fold zeroing... zero via separate micro-phase before GEMV phase (barrier) OR initialize by having tile (k_chunk==0) WRITE and others ADD — race (others may add first). Use atomicAdd everywhere after a zeroing phase — zeroing 2304 floats × slots: trivial; merge zeroing into previous phase (any CTA can zero scratch during preceding phase work). OK. Actually with atomicAdd fp32 for split-K, values are per-element sums of ≤8 chunk partials — deterministic-ish enough. Bandwidth accounting demands MOST CTAs busy during the big phases (qkvg gate/up down, attention for MLA at long ctx). Small phases (conv/S: 12MB, o_proj 4.7MB, router) will underutilize — that's where latency hides. Small phases total bytes maybe 20MB of 240MB → 11µs at poor utilization ~30µs of wall — acceptable. Could also OVERLAP phases: work-items of independent phases fused into one mega-phase? E.g., o_proj (after conv/S) is independent of nothing else in that barrier window... hmm; gate/up GEMV could start before... no, sequential deps. Better idea to keep utilization: choose tile sizes per phase so that #tiles ≥ grid (or grid-stride with dynamic... static assignment suffices: work_item = flattened tile index; each CTA processes items pid, pid+grid, ...). For small phases, increase parallelism: conv/S phase: 3 layers?? NO — layers are sequential (block i output feeds i+1). Within one KDA: conv 4096 + S 16K×32... S update tiles: (32 heads × 128 dv each... make tiles (h, 32-dv) = 128 tiles × work(128 rows × 32 cols... reads S 128×32×4=16KB+writes) → 128 work items + conv 4096/256 = 16 → ~144. OK-ish (188 grid → 76% used). o_proj: split-K 4 × N64: 2304/64=36 × 4 = 144 tiles → ok. qkvg GEMV: BN 128: (16384/128)=128 tiles... make BN 64 → 256 tiles. Each tile: 64 cols × 2304 K: 73.7KB int4 + scales. 256 tiles → good. But BN too small hurts per-tile efficiency (x smem reload per tile small anyway). Try BN=128 → 128 tiles × 188: each CTA ≤1 tile, 68 CTAs idle. BN=64: 256 tiles → 68 CTAs get 2... hmm 256 = 188+68: unbalanced tail (31% over). BN=96? Sizes... grid-stride absorbs: total work 16384 cols; per CTA 87 cols; tail raggedness ~ 50% of one tile per CTA ≈ acceptable imbalance (phase time = max CTA = 2 tiles = 2×min). Tail imbalance wastes ~30% of that phase. Hmm. Alternatively K-split too: flatten (col-tile, k-chunk): total (16384/128)×(2304/512→ 4.5→5? ragged K)... K=2304 → 4 chunks of 576: 128×4 = 512 atomic tiles → each CTA ~2.7 tiles → imbalance 1/3.7 ≈ 27% same. Eh. With BN=128 exactly 128 tiles for KDA-in phase: utilization 128/188 = 68% but each tile is BIG (128 cols × 2304 = 147KB int4). Phase = 24.7MB total... at 68% BW ≈ 24.7/(0.68×1.7) = 21µs vs ideal 14.5µs. Better: BN=64→256 tiles: 24.7/(0.97×1.7) = 15µs. But dequant efficiency at BN=64: each row load = 64B/warp... 2 sectors fine. I should stop hand-wringing and consider that phases can be MERGED when independent: KDA qkvg GEMV (24.7MB) + nothing else... it's block-start; previous phase = MoE down of PREVIOUS block + residual → barrier → qkvg. So phases strictly serialize around tiny ones. Estimated step: Per KDA block: qkvg 15µs, conv/S ~4MB → ~3µs (util 76%), o_proj+resid 4.7MB → util 144/188=77% → 3.6µs, MoE g/u (9 experts fused silu: gate 2304→1024, up same; per expert 2×1.18MB; 9×2.36 = 21.2MB; tiles: 9 experts × (2048/128)=16 → 144 tiles…77% util → 14.8µs → say 15µs), down (9×1.18=10.6MB, tiles 9×(2304/128)=162 →86% → 7.3µs) + barriers 4×1.5=6µs + norms etc. KDA block ≈ 15+3+3.6+15+7.3+6 ≈ 50µs. Hmm vs pure-BW bound (58MB→34µs): overhead +12µs/block. ×3 KDA = 150µs. MLA: weights 14.6+cache 19+MoE 33 = ~67MB → 40µs ideal, overheads → ~55µs. Total ≈ 205µs → 4800 tok/s? Speedup vs baseline (~1.6ms@2k, ~3ms@16k?) ≈ 10-15×. Improvement ideas later (fusion of small phases via specialized work assignment, skipping zeroing phases, barrier latency tuning, 2 CTAs/SM). First get it CORRECT, then profile. Alright, let's also think about the flip side: maybe ONE persistent CTA per SM with 512-1024 threads is inflexible; grid = 188. Or grid = 376 (2/SM, smem ≤ 100KB each). Start: grid = #SMs, num_warps = 8 (256 threads) — Triton max num_warps? 32 (1024 threads) allowed. More threads per CTA = more parallel loads = better BW per CTA; but barrier arrival granularity coarser (fewer CTAs = cheaper barrier). Let me start 188 CTAs × 8 warps? Hmm 256 threads × 188 = 48K threads; each issuing ~8-16 outstanding 16B loads → 12MB in flight... enough. Tune later. Now — big practical question: how do I pass ~40 tensors to a Triton kernel cleanly? Just list them as args; Triton handles it. Use tl.constexpr ints for offsets/counts. L (context len) runtime arg. Barrier pointer + phase counter base arg. Let me also reconsider prep of weights (lazy, one-time per model after load_state_dict): For each QuantLinear: given w_q (K/2, N) uint8, scales (G,N) bf16, zeros (G,N) bf16. Decide kernel layout: I'll keep w_q AS-IS (K/2, N) and have tiles be (BK2 packed-rows × BN cols). Actually wait, reconsider vectorization: tile (BK2, BN): memory layout: row k2 stride = N; within row, BN contiguous bytes. Triton assigns threads: canonical order — last dim contiguous across threads. With BLOCK (64, 64): elements = 4096 bytes → per row 64 bytes = 64 threads × 1B or 16 thr × 4B... Triton picks vector width from contiguity & divisibility: 1-byte elements → can vectorize to 4/8/16 bytes per thread along N (alignment: N multiple of 16, offsets k2*N — fine). It'll emit LDG.128 for uint8 when ≥16B/thread along contiguous dim. OK trust Triton; validate with ncu later. Scales access pattern: for K-rows within group... in tile (BK2=64 → k-range 128 = one group g): load s (BN,) slice, z (BN,) → good. Plan the kernel phases concretely. Constants: H=2304, C=4096, HEADS=32, DK=128, KV=512, QR=64, QK=192, VH=128, MO=1024, E=64. Per decode step, static work (all phases, grid-stride, BARRIER between): Phase P0 (per block i in 0..3): attn-input GEMVs. - Block kind K: compute y = GEMV4(x_norm) covering q,k,v,g → 16384 cols, in=2304. Work items: tiles (BN×BK). Write bf16? fp32 to scratch q,k,v,g (fp32; conv needs fp32... conv stores bf16 window and val; q/k/v post-silu → fp32 for S math). Store conv-window new rows: val bf16 is needed for windows (win row2 = val bf16). Store both fp32 scratch AND bf16 window-appends? The conv phase can write window rows itself: it has val... no wait conv phase has q/k/v (they are what GEMV writes). Let conv phase handle window update (needs val = GEMV output as bf16-rounded? Reference stores into window the bf16 q_proj output (post-rounding). Conv reads window bf16 rows 0,1 and val bf16. To match: conv phase reads GEMV fp32 outputs, rounds val to bf16 for window row write, uses bf16 values in conv (win rows 0,1,2 are bf16! win = cat([prev(bf16), val(bf16)]); conv in fp32 on bf16 inputs). So conv phase: read win rows 0-1 (bf16), val fp32→bf16; compute conv fp32; write window rows (shift: row0=old row1... just write row2? window is (3,C): new window = [old1, old2, val]: in-place shift in the SAME tensor: thread reads r0..r2? careful: write row0=old row1 while another thread might read row0... each (c) column handled by one thread: reads 4 vals, writes 3 regs→rows 0,1,2. One thread per channel → no race. conv tiles: C channels / (256 thr) = 16 tiles. - Block kind M: q_proj GEMV → 6144 cols (in 2304); kv_a → 576 cols: flatten tiles. Write fp32 scratch. Total 6720 cols. Then P1: per kind: - K: conv+qkv prep: (i) conv for q,k,v + silu → qk,v fp32 scratch (scaled q ×scale), (ii) g = -softplus(g_raw) → exp table? store gexp = exp(-softplus(graw)) fp32 (4096), (iii) beta = sigmoid(beta_proj(x_norm)) (32 cols — beta GEMV in this phase; input x_norm from smem/global — it's only 2304×32 bf16 = 147KB weight — assign a few lanes/tile). Barrier covers S-update needs. - K: S update + o: tiles (h, dv 32): 128 tiles → o fp32 (4096). Wait — barrier between conv and S? Both in P1? S update needs conv OUTPUT (k,v) and gexp and beta — so conv must complete first. Could fuse: S-tile (h,dv32) needs k[h],v[h],q[h],g[h],beta[h] — 5×128... those are produced by conv phase. If same barrier phase, race. SPLIT: P1 = conv+prep; P2 = S update + o. OR: merge conv into S phase: tile (h, dv32) computes conv for q,k,v of head h itself (3×128 channels, reads windows 4×... per head: win slices (4,128)×3, conv weights (128×4)×3): compute conv+silu in-tile, THEN S update. Also gexp[h] computed in-tile (128 values: needs g_raw[h] fp32 — from GEMV scratch): exp compute per tile. beta[h]: needs beta GEMV (2304×32 entire row for ONE scalar per head — recomputing per tile = 2304 MACs per tile... 128 tiles × 32 heads?? beta[h] for head h: per-tile cost 2304-FMA GEMV-column → 128 tiles × 2304 = 294K MAC — fine honestly, but weight read 9.2KB ×128 = 1.2MB L2 — fine). And window WRITE-BACK must happen exactly once: let tile (h, 0..) i.e., dv-chunk 0 of each head also write the shifted window. WIN: one less barrier per KDA layer. P1(K) = [conv+S+o fused, tiles (h,dv32) = 128 + extra tiles could cover nothing else]. Work per tile: conv (3 ch × 128 × 4 FMA) + S (128 dk × 32 dv × ~8 flops = 32K flops): reads S 16KB fp32, writes 16KB. 128 tiles × (32KB r+w) = 4MB... plus q expansion: dot for each dv: Σ_dk S[dk,dv]*q[dk] needs q[h] 128 in smem/regs. OK. Hmm wait, one problem: conv writes window updates (rows) — only tile dv0 per head writes → tiles dv0 do double duty; fine. Oh but another subtlety: GEMV P0 writes q,k,v,g to scratch as fp32; P1 reads them (after barrier). Good. - M: P1 = rope + cache append (+ optional copy if fresh): tiles: q_rope (32 heads × 64 → few tiles), k_rope new token (1×64), cache write rows L (576). Copy phase if marker absent: L×576 elements / (256×...) — grid-stride copy tiles. P2(M) = absorb q: q_abs[h,j] = Σ_d q_nope[h,d] × Wq_k[d... wait kv_b weight layout: kv_b: QuantLinear(512, 8192). w_q (256, 8192): packed along in (512→256 bytes), out 8192 = per head 256 cols: cols h*256 + (0..127 k_nope, 128..255 v). q_abs[h,j]: j in 512 (k-dim of latent): q_abs[h,j] = Σ_{d=0..127} q_nope[h,d] * W_kv[j, h*256 + d]. This is a small GEMM per head: (1×128)×(128×512... no: for head h: W_k^h is (512, 128) slice → q_abs[h] (512) = W_k^h^T... q_abs[j] = Σ_d W[j,d]*q[d] — GEMV over the TRANSPOSE of the weight — weight access along its in-dim j, output j... i.e., out features = 512 (j), in = 128 (d), weight = dequant(kv_b)[j, h*256+d]. So 32 GEMVs of (128→512) with weight slice columns. Work: 32×512 outs = 16384 outs × 128 MACs = 2M FLOP; weight bytes 2.1MB. Tiles: BN... treat like GEMV with K=128, N=16384 (offset per head). K=128 = 64 packed rows, ONE group (group 128: g = 0..3? kv_b in=512 → 4 groups; K-slice here is j (in-dim of kv_b) — wait no. q_abs[j]: j indexes kv_b's IN dimension (512 dim, the rows!); d indexes kv_b's OUT columns. GEMV over out-cols... the weight tile for output j: row j of W = packed byte index j//2, col h*256+d. So q_abs computation reads FULL kv_b weight columns h*256+d (d≤127) — i.e., the k_nope half (2.1MB/2 = 1.05MB) in K-strided pattern. Layout (K/2=256 rows, N=8192): q_abs[h,j] = Σ_d W_p dequant [j, h*256+d] — reduction over COLUMNS d — transposed GEMV = reduction along N dim → awkward for memory coalescing (row-major: along d contiguous! Want tiles: fix j-range, loop d: w[j, d] contiguous in d → coalesced along d, accumulate into q_abs[h, j] — partial over... reduction along d: assign tile = (h, j-chunk 64?) × full d=128: per tile: 64 rows × 128 cols: 64×64B packed... per thread: row j: read 64 bytes (128 nibbles over d=128) contiguous, dot with q_nope[h, d] (128 floats smem). 32 heads × 512/64 = 256 tiles. Hmm this is getting complicated but fine — it's a "transposed" GEMV pattern; also the o-side output projection has the same shape (ctx[h,j] × W[j, h*256+128+d] summed over j → out d): NORMAL GEMV (reduction over j rows — standard). P2b? MLA attention needs q_abs complete → barrier. P3 = online-softmax attention over cache chunks (tiles: position-chunks; grid-stride; per-chunk partials to scratch). Barriers. P4 = combine partials (small: one/few CTAs — combine NCHUNK partials per head) → ctx (32×512) fp32... need ALL chunks combined before output proj. P4 also computes... P5 = output absorb proj: o[h,d128] = Σ_j ctx[h,j] W_v[j] (+ then o bf16) → tiles (h, d...): reads W v-half 1.05MB + gather → o (4096). P6 = o_proj GEMV + residual. Barriers in MLA: P0→P1 (GEMV done), P1→P2 (rope/append ready? P2 needs q_nope from P0 — the barrier after P0 covers), P2→P3 (q_abs ready), P3→P4 (partials ready), P4→P5 (ctx ready), P5→P6 (o ready), P6→P7 MoE. That's a lot of barriers — 7 in MLA! Can compress: P1(rope/append) merge into P2? rope affects q_rope (P3 needs) and cache row L (P3 reads)... P2 computing q_abs doesn't need rope; P3 needs cache+rope+q_abs. So merge rope/append INTO P2 phase (different tiles in same phase): P2 = [absorb GEMV tiles] + [rope/append tiles] → saves 1 barrier. P4 combine (small) merge into P5? P5 tiles each need full ctx — no. Keep. 6 barriers MLA, 4-5 per KDA (P0,P1,P2 oproj, P3 MoEgu, P4 down → 4 barriers + block-boundary = 5). Total barriers ≈ 3×5 + 7 ≈ 22 + final ~ 23. Barrier cost on RTX PRO: measure later; assume 1-2µs → 25-45µs total. Hmm — that's my biggest latency risk after BW efficiency. Alternative to full-grid barriers: could restrict barrier participation... Actually here's a trick: PERSISTENT-DOUBLE-PUMP: since we have 188 CTAs, barrier = all CTAs atomically increment; last one flips release flag (write phase id); spinners read release via LDG volatile (poll interval ~100-200ns with __nanosleep). ~1µs achievable (seen in NCCL/one-shot: ~1.5-3µs actual). 23 barriers × 2µs = 46µs — 20% overhead. Ugh. But wait — barrier latency overlaps with DRAIN: CTAs arrive skewed; the barrier cost beyond work-imbalance is ~ release propagation ≈ 0.5-1µs. Fine, accept; optimize later if dominant. Also NOTE: with only ~90-220 MB streaming per step and 1.8TB/s, absolute times are tiny (~100µs); benchmark steps: 32 steps ctx2048 → fine. Let me now also plan: per-CTA static work assignment: keep a global "work counter"? No — deterministic: each phase has total NT tiles; CTA pid handles tiles pid, pid+G, pid+2G... — simple static grid-stride. No atomics needed beyond barrier. Register pressure: phases differ hugely (GEMV wants many accumulators). Triton compiles the whole kernel with max register need across phases → occupancy limited by worst phase. With grid=188 1 CTA/SM, occupancy 1 CTA/SM guaranteed as long as regs ≤ 65536/block... 256 threads × 255 regs = 65K — fine at num_warps=8. Smem: Triton allocates max across program — GEMV tiles use smem for x + weights staging; static smem on Blackwell = 227KB/SM max; fine. Instruction cache: giant kernel — phases × code size; i-cache ~32-64KB... giant unrolled loops could thrash; keep loops modestly unrolled. Triton generates reasonable code. OK — and the "absorb" decision also applies to kv_b: we dequant kv_b TWICE (once for q_abs absorb, once for the v-side output projection): weight int4 traffic 2×2.1MB = 4.2MB + scales. Alternatively dequant once into smem?? 32 heads × 512 × 256... 8.4MB bf16 — no. Registers? No. Global scratch: write 8.4MB + read 8.4MB vs re-read int4 2.1MB (+unpack ALU ×2) — re-read wins. Wait, actually can I avoid the transposed GEMV (q_abs) by REFORMULATING? scores[h,l] = Σ_d q_nope[h,d]·k_nope[l,h,d]... the alternative "lazy" computation: for each cache position l, compute k_nope[l,h,d] on the fly?? that's the materialize path (L×32×256×512 FLOPs = way too much). No — q_abs transposed GEMV is right. Hmm, one more idea for q_abs GEMV: it's exactly equivalent to einsum('hd,hjd->hj') where W[h,j,d] = kv_b_deq[j, h*256+d]. Batch-GEMV over heads: for head h: W_h (512×128) — reduction over d (columns, contiguous!) means per output j we reduce along the contiguous dim — coalescing good but reduction-along-contiguous forces intra-thread accumulation: thread handling row j reads 64B (its d=0..127 nibbles) sequentially → dot with q_nope[h] — per-thread sequential 64B loads → uncoalesced-ish but each is 64B contiguous → 2×32B sectors per thread — coalescing ACROSS threads poor (thread j reads different 64B region). Warp of 32 threads reading 32 different rows: 32×64B = 2KB scattered → each 64B = 2 sectors fully utilized! So DRAM efficiency OK (sectors fully used), request count 2/thread. FINE. And o-side: o[h,d] = Σ_j ctx[h,j] W[j, h*256+128+d]: reduction over j (rows, strided): standard GEMV tiling: tiles (d-chunk × j-chunk), accumulate via atomicAdd fp32 (j-chunks split) or full-j per tile: K=512 (256 packed rows) small enough: tile = (h, 128d = all) → 32 tiles?? too few. tile = (h, 32 d) = 128 tiles: each reads 256 rows × 32B = 8KB + ctx[h] 2KB. Good: 128 tiles × 8KB = 1.05MB. Then o_proj normal GEMV (4096→2304, split-K). Let me also think about whether o_proj + residual + next-block rmsnorm... residual: y = x + o_proj out: y write hidden. rmsnorm(y) needs full y; P(MoE) computes rmsnorm redundantly per CTA (2304 reads). Fine. And the FINAL output of step: after block 3's MoE-down+residual phase, write hidden_out tensor (2304 bf16). The same phase writes it (residual sum) — no extra barrier (returned after kernel completes). Starting state prep per launch: hidden input: h (bf16, 2304) → x. Phase P0 needs rmsnorm(x, attn_norm0) — computed redundantly per CTA from global h + norm weight (from aux blob). Now, precision: x_norm values per CTA computed identically? Each CTA sums 2304 squares → float; same order → same result (deterministic if same code path: each CTA runs identical loop → identical). RMSNorm must match reference: mean = Σx²/2304 (fp32), rsqrt, ×w(bf16→fp32), result... reference _rmsnorm returns (xf*w).to(bf16)! So normed x is bf16! GEMV inputs bf16-rounded. Match: our GEMV activation operands = bf16(x_norm)... then GEMV accum fp32 of bf16×dequant — matches reference path (x.float()@(w.float())). Our dequant of scales/zeros bf16→fp32 exact. q_fp32 accumulate → round bf16 at store to scratch? Reference q_proj output bf16 (then conv window stores it, float() for conv). I'll store fp32 scratch from GEMV but ALSO window needs bf16(val): val_bf16 = bf16(fp32acc) — equals reference exactly (assuming accumulation order match — not guaranteed to be bit-equal! cublas order unknown. But close enough for cosine). OK. Let me now write down the full work-item table. Define helper: phase loop: ``` for phase in range(NPHASE): # unrolled? phases differ in code → use if chains; loop var must be constexpr-unrollable → write explicit blocks separated by barrier() ``` Actually cleaner: write straight-line code: ``` # block 0..3 via runtime loop for i in tl.static_range(4)? kinds differ (K vs M) → branch on i<3 static. ``` Hmm, kinds: pattern ("K","K","K","M") static. Write straight-line: kda_block(b0_ptrs...), kda_block(...), kda_block(...), mla_block(...), each ending without barrier; barriers embedded. To reduce code duplication via @triton.jit helper functions (inlined) — fine. But kernel ARG LIST: all blocks' weights — pass flat blobs + constexpr offsets. Plan blobs: BLOB A: `wq_all` uint8: concatenation of all packed weights in fixed order. But wait — w_q for each weight comes in as separate tensors in state_dict; in prepare() I copy them into one big flat uint8 tensor (torch.cat → that's a kernel but one-time prepare). Then per-weight offset (bytes) = python int → constexpr. BLOB B: `sz_all` bf16: concatenated scales+zeros pairs. Per weight: scales (G,N) then zeros (G,N) contiguous. Offset in elements. BLOB C: `aux_all` bf16: conv_w (3,C,4 per KDA block), beta_w (32,2304) per KDA, router_w (64,2304) per block, norms attn_norm/moe_norm (2304) per block. BLOB S: scratch fp32: x(2304), h(2304+pad), normed not needed (computed per-CTA), q/raw qkv g (4096×4), mla_q (6144), kv_new (576), q_slow... let me enumerate: - xn: current normed? no — computed in-register per CTA. - qkv blocks: q_raw,k_raw,v_raw (4096 each fp32 — raw GEMV outs), g_raw (4096); after conv: q_s (4096), k_s, v_s, gexp: can reuse IN PLACE? conv for channel c reads raw[c] only... but window update: win rows already stored separately... wait, conv needs RAW val[c] and prev window rows; conv output → overwrite raw[c] in place? YES safe per-channel (each channel reads raw[c] once, writes conv-out[c]) — but the NEW window row = raw val bf16 — write into state window buffer row2 after shifting rows (in-place per-channel ops on state tensor + scratch — single-thread-per-channel → safe). gexp: in-place on g_raw. - o (4096 fp32), attn-proj out accum (2304 fp32 for split-K atomic — need zeroing! who zeroes? The PREVIOUS phase that has spare tiles... zero 2304 floats = 9 tiles of 256: schedule as extra work items in P2 (S-update phase: 128+9=137 tiles). Hmm zero-before-needed: P3 does atomicAdd into oproj_acc → must be zeroed AFTER P2?? P3 starts after barrier; zero in P2 — safe since nothing touches oproj_acc before P3. - moe_h (9×1024 fp32 silu-gated), moe_acc (2304 fp32 atomic split... down GEMV: each tile = (expert e, n-chunk): accumulate w_e × down into moe_acc[n]: atomicAdd; zeroed in P3. - router scratch: logits (64 fp32) + topk ids (8 int) + weights (8 f32) + done flag. - MLA: kv (576 f32), q_abs (32×512 f32 = 64KB), rope'd q_rope in place of mla_q rope part? q from q_proj: q_nope part (fp32 scratch) feeds absorb GEMV; q_rope part needs rope then attention. Store rope output to scratch (32×64 f32). - attn partials: part_m (CH×32), part_l (CH×32), part_acc (CH×32×512 f32). CH = number of position-chunks. Parallelize attention at fine granularity for L=16384: chunk=256 positions → 64 chunks × 32 heads: per-chunk work: 256×576×2 bytes = 288KB reads. Total 18.9MB. 64 chunks → 64 tiles → util 34%!! At ctx 16k the attention phase is 19MB — 11µs ideal, util-limited to ~3× → BAD.
