Prompt EXPLICITLY allows: "one CUDA __global__ built with load_inline" ✓. Framework label in check.py: "cuda_raw" via regex load_inline ✓ fine. OK — FULL PLAN SET: CUDA C++ megakernel. Now design the kernel precisely. ## CUDA megakernel design Grid: G = SMs × occ (query occupancy: target 1 CTA/SM × 256 threads? or 512 threads?). More threads/CTA = more streams for BW. Registers: GEMV needs ~40-60 regs; attention chunk ~100; S-update small. Choose 256 threads (8 warps... CUDA block 256) grid = 114 = SMs. If occupancy says 2+ CTAs/SM possible with ≤ some regs — but grid.sync() works with ANY grid ≤ resident capacity: pick grid = 114 (safely 1/SM). Later try 228 with careful reg budget (grid-stride code handles any G). Hmm — actually with 256 threads/SM, SM BW potential: 256 threads × ~8 outstanding uint32 (4B) = 8KB in flight/SM → 114 × 8KB = 0.9MB — short of 1.4MB → BW ~ 60-70%?? With unroll×16: 1.8MB ✓. Fine-tune: 512 threads/CTA (16 warps): same regs/thread ~ 64 → 32K regs... 512×64 = 32K ≤ 64K ✓ smem ~ x slice 9KB + reduction buffers small ✓ 1 CTA/SM ✓ occupancy fine. 512 threads × 8 loads = 16KB/SM in flight → 1.8MB ✓✓ better. Use 512 threads? But GEMV tile = 128 cols/warp → 16 warps: fine regardless. Barrier arrival count per CTA = 1 (grid.sync syncs THREADS via barrier at CTA too ✓ cg::grid_group::sync). grid.sync() latency ~ few µs at 114 CTAs... similar to spin; measure. ALTERNATIVE: cooperative launch + MY barriers (cheaper? cg grid.sync is implemented as ~ my arrive/spin). Just use grid.sync() — wait, one gotcha: grid.sync() requires ALL THREADS to call it (converged). With divergent phase code (some warps idle at phase end) all still call ✓ by construction (phase loops then grid.sync()). Threads: 512/CTA → GEMV: warp per 128-col tile ✓ works. ### Phase structures in CUDA (block = 512 threads, 16 warps) Common helpers: - `xw_norm(xptr, normptr, rstd_out)`: cooperative: threads sum x² → block reduce (smem) → rstd broadcast. ~21 loads... 2304 elems / 512 thr: each thread loads ~4.5 → block-reduce via smem tree (2 rounds) ~ fast. - GEMV tiles: as designed: warp-owned 128-col full-K tiles over a tile table; phases differ in (in,out) per matrix; tile→matrix mapping via a small CONSTEXPR table passed... runtime table in constant memory/params struct! Pass a `Params` struct by VALUE (kernel arg can be a struct — CUDA supports const params). With ~100 int64 offsets + ints ≈ 1KB param — kernel constant bank max 4KB (32KB on newer) ✓ CUDA kernel params up to 32KB on sm_70+... PTX limit 64KB? CUDA 12.1+ allows 32KB... our struct ~1-2KB ✓ FINE. All offsets/addresses INSIDE struct (no pointer-table memory needed!). Kernel signature: `__global__ void mega(Params P)` — single arg ✓✓. Params contents: base pointers: WB, SB, AB, SC (scratch), BAR?, (grid.sync needs no scratch!), x_in, x_out, state ptrs (S[3], cq/ck/cv[3] each 3 tensors... 12 ptrs), ckv_src, kr_src, ws_ckv, ws_kr, L, fresh, + per-matrix GEMV descriptors? GEMV tables are STATIC (sizes known) → HARDCODE offsets as compile-time constants generated... hmm offsets depend on tensor sizes (static!) → COMPUTE OFFSETS AT COMPILE TIME: I'll bake the layout as #defines in the CUDA source (generated by Python once — careful: "runtime codegen" is a red flag?? NO — prepare() computing offsets at RUNTIME is data, not codegen. Baking offsets into source via Python _SOURCE_GENERATION_ during development (I write the .cu source with the constants final) ✓ — the source is static text in solution.py (constants computed by me, written literally or as constexpr arithmetic in the .cu from base dims!! offsets = f(H, C, ...) — write offset formulas in C++ constexpr from the dims ✓✓ ZERO python codegen - clean). So .cu has `constexpr` offset computation from dims; Python passes ONLY the tensor pointers + L + gen + fresh flag as Params. Scratch layout (fp32 SC): constexpr offsets too. ### GEMV tile table phases (v1) — signed off earlier with warp=128-col tiles: P0 KDA: matrices q,k,v,g: 4×4096 = 16384 cols → 128 tiles → warps: 114×16 = 1824 warp-slots ≥ 128 ✓ each warp ≤1 tile ✓ (util 128/1824... WARP util 7%?!?! NO WAIT — the TILE is processed by one warp looping full-K; 128 tiles × 1 warp = 128 warps busy of 1824 = 7%!!! The whole DRAM must be fed by 128 warps × 32 lanes × ~8 in flight × 4B = 131KB in flight — 10x SHORT!! DISASTER. Warp-per-tile is wrong scale. RETHINK: need ~29K threads × 8 × 4B ≈ 1MB in flight. So tiles must be processed by MANY warps: 128-col tile by ONE warp looping 1152 rows: that warp's 32 lanes pull 4B each per row-iter = 128B/iter; 16 warps of a CTA on 16 tiles. To engage ALL 1824 warps: total warp-work must be ~1824 items: make tiles = (128 cols × k-chunk): k-chunk = 128 rows (256 k, 2 groups): tile = 128cols × 128 rows: bytes = 16KB: total tiles for KDA P0 = (16384/128) × (1152/128) = 128 × 9 = 1152 warp-tiles ✓ 63% util one wave — and PARTIAL sums per k-chunk → ATOMIC or 2-stage reduce... AtomicAdd fp32 partials into y: 16384 cols × 9 chunks... = y element atomics 16384... wait PER TILE warp produces 128 partial values → atomicAdd 128 floats × 1152 tiles = 147K atomic-adds of 4B = 590KB atomics per such phase ✓ fine (L2 atomic throughput ~ 10-20 GB/s... hmm 590KB / phase at say 1µs — ok). Hmm, OR hierarchical: k-chunks mapped WITHIN warp... no. Zeroing y scratch before phase: 16384 fp32 zero = 64KB — any phase-earlier CTAs (plenty idle in P7/P8) can zero NEXT phase's buffers: e.g., during P8/prior-block phases. Simplest: at the END of the kernel (last phase), zero ALL accum buffers for NEXT launch — no wait, y accum used THIS launch must be zeroed AFTER last use and BEFORE next use — next use = next launch's P0 → zero at end of THIS launch ✓ works across launches (incl. first: zero at prepare time with cudaMemsetAsync — a memset op (allowed? it's not a compute kernel; cudaMemsetAsync on stream IS technically a device operation — do it during PREPARE only, not in step ✓; then kernel self-maintains zero state ✓). Alternatively skip-atomic design: cross-warp reduction via smem per CTA: k-chunks assigned WITHIN a CTA for a given colrange... e.g., CTA-tile = 128 cols × full-K with 16 warps splitting K: each warp takes k-chunk (K/16 → 144 rows → ragged; K=2304: K2=1152 rows/16 = 72 rows = 1152 k?? rows per warp 72 hmm want group alignment: 64 rows/group → warp k-chunks of 64·w? 1152 = 16×72 — 72 rows = 1.125 groups MISALIGNED!! pad: warp k-range 64-row units: 18 units → 16 warps take 1-2 units (ragged ✓ loop). partial acc per warp in SMEM: 128 cols × 16 warps fp32 = 8KB smem ✓ then ONE warp reduces 16×128 → writes y. CTA-tile = 128 cols: P0 tiles = 128 CTA-tiles → 114 CTAs ~1.1 waves — 90% util one-wave-ish + NO atomics/zeroing!! ✓✓ I like it: CTA-tile unit of work, internal k-split across warps, smem reduce. k-unit = 64 rows (1 group, clean s/z): each warp: for its k-unit: 64 rows × 128B... warp processes rows sequentially 64 iterations... 64 iters × 4B/lane loads: unroll 4-8 → outstanding ok. (y = rstd·y_raw ✓ since rstd scalar)). Barrier count: KDA×3: P0-P4 + blocksync = 5×... precisely: sync after P0, P1, P2, P3, P4(=blocksync) = 5 per KDA = 15; MLA: P0,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 + P8 = 9 hmm: sync after each + P8-tail... P8 can share the P7 sync: order: P7 down(atomics) | sync | P8 (read MOEACC write hidden_out bf16 2304 + zero counters) — 1 sync after P7 ✓ 9 syncs for MLA. TOTAL 24 grid.syncs. Others: smem reduction scratch for GEMV: 16 warps × 128 cols × 4B = 8KB ✓. Actually wait — GEMV k-split smem reduce: 16 warps each hold partial acc for SAME 128 cols: warp w's lanes: lane t cols... every warp has SAME col-assignment (4 cols per lane t) but different k-chunks ✓: partials smem[w][128]: 16×512B?? 128 cols ×4B = 512B per warp → 8KB ✓ reduce: warp0: per col: Σ16 — or ALL warps: col c: owned by... final y: assign each warp 8 cols: warp0..15: cols 8w..8w+8: lanes 0-7 each one col: reads 16 partials → sum → y → apply residual/store ✓ (~16 ld.shared per lane — banked fine). ### End-of-step: hidden_out bf16 written P8 from MOEACC fp32 ✓ return persistent buffer; ALSO moeacc→bf16 rounding for NEXT step's P0 rmsnorm: reads MOEACC fp32 + rounds per element (as established) ✓. ### zero-work counters: P8 item ✓ (first launch: prepare does cudaMemset on counters + attn partials? partials self-scoped ✓ only counters need initial zero → memset at prepare ✓). ### VERY IMPORTANT state mgmt recap on host `step()`: ``` if not prepared: prepare() mla entry: m = state[mla_idx] if m.get("_ws", None) is ws_c_view_marker... fresh = 0; src ptrs = ws ptrs (m["c_kv"] already view) else: fresh = 1; src ptrs = m["c_kv"].data_ptr()... L = m["c_kv"].shape[0] set Params fields launch if fresh: m["c_kv"] = ws_c[:L+1]; m["k_rope"] = ws_k[:L+1]; m["_ws"] = self._ws_marker else: m["c_kv"] = ws_c[:L+1]... (same base) just re-slice (new view objects) KDA states: pass S/cq/ck/cv pointers directly (in-place update) ✓ unchanged dict entries. return hid_buf, state ``` Note: `m["c_kv"] = ws_c[:L+1]` — view of OUR buffer; check.py flattens ✓. hmm: `init_state` provides c_kv (L,512) — L = context_len; benchmark passes across steps ✓ shapes grow ws_c[:L+1+i...] ✓ L read from shape each step ✓ ws capacity 16384+64: for ctx=16384 & 16 steps: 16400 ≤ cap 16448 ✓ make 16512. Also VERIFY check.py's `mla_cache` cosine uses st_sol[mla_layer]["c_kv"] — values: rows 0..L' — in fresh case copied from source (exact bf16 copies ✓) + our new row ✓ cosine ✓. ### State pointers as kernel args: Params with all pointers — 24ish pointers + L + fresh ≈ 220B ✓. ### Preparing weights for the GEMV pattern: NO repack at all?! (K/2,N) layout with lane=4cols(uint32) reads row segments ✓ original layout works!! Repack = pure CONCATENATION into blobs WB/SB/AB (+ pad N to multiple of 128? — GEMV-tiles along N: N=576 kv_a: 576 = 4×128 + 64: last tile partial: either pad blob (pad cols with anything; y ignored if we never read those cols... uv: write y for padded cols into... y scratch per matrix sized separately — store y per-matrix at its scratch offset with N real cols; tile covering cols 512..639 writes 512..575 real + 576..639 → scratch must have padding space (allocate scratch with N padded) ✓ NO weight padding needed as long as SCRATCH is padded and WEIGHT reads stay in-bounds: weight rows have N=576 bytes; tile reading cols 512..639 READS OUT OF ROW into next row's data (blob contiguous!) → values garbage in y[576..639] — UNUSED ✓ but memory-safe?? read beyond logical row is fine within blob (only last matrix's last row could overflow the WHOLE blob by up to 127 bytes → PAD BLOB by 128B at end ✓). s/z reads similarly (they're sized (g, N) rows in blob — same overflow-safe ✓ pad 128 elements... careful: s strides by N → offsets per matrix in blob: I'll store each matrix's s/z as (G, N) contiguous blocks ✓ same out-of-row tolerance ✓.) ✓ just pad blob ends by 1KB. ### Now — CUDA launch mechanics via torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline: - functions: `launch_mega(Params...)` — passing struct from Python: instead, expose `megastep(tensors..., L, fresh)` C++ function taking torch::Tensors: it fills Params and calls cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel ✓ returns nothing. Python passes ~20 tensors per call (~2-3µs overhead fine... actually 20 tensor arg unpack ~ big-ish; do: pass tensors ONCE at prepare (store refs in a C++-side registry?? simpler: pass every time, tens or so — measure overhead later; could be ~5-10µs of PYTHON-C++ marshalling — hmm that's real money at 160µs target. For cooperative: cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor(kernel, 512, smem_static?) → occ; G = 114×occ. start 512 threads... try 256 too. ### One more V1-scope decision: attention smem staging with cp.async not needed v1 (sync loads ok). I think the design is solid. TOO MUCH?? P0' tiles NEED rstd only at the END (apply post-scale at store!! y = rstd·y_raw ✓✓): EVERY tile: does full un-scaled accumulation then needs rstd → spin-flag per phase?! UGH. assistant

Triton tops out ~0.66 TB/s for the fused GEMV (byte-extract/convert chains). The design that hits full BW (thread-owns-4-columns + uint32 loads + magic-bias unpack) needs CUDA-level control. So the megakernel will be CUDA C++ via load_inline with a cooperative launch (guaranteed co-residency for grid-wide spin barriers). Let me verify cooperative launch works through load_inline.

CTA-tile = 128 cols of one matrix. 16 warps split K. Each warp: k-range subset in units of GROUPS (128 k = 64 packed rows). K2 rows total (e.g. 1152 for K=2304; groups = 18). Warps get groups: warp w takes groups w, w+16, ... For K=2304: groups 18 → warps 0..15 get 1-2 groups. Per group: 64 rows. Per row: uint32 load (4 cols), 8 nibbles, x broadcast ×2, 8 FMA(+magic-sub). Per group per lane: s,z for its 4 cols: acc fold. Per-lane registers: dot[4], yacc[4], xsum (per group), ~13 + addresses ~ 25-35 total. smem: partial[warp][128] fp32 = 8KB. Reduce at end of tile: warps write partials, sync, warp... final: each warp takes 8 columns (4 lanes × ... hmm 128 cols / 16 warps = 8 cols/warp: lanes 0-7 of each warp... use 2 lanes per... simple: for c in 0..7: col = w*8+c: lane<8 sums 16 partials → out. But broadcast loads of 16 partials per lane — fine. Store: y value = rstd * y_raw (for normed GEMV) or plain; store to scratch fp32 (or bf16 — decide at use site: I'll store fp32 into raw scratch; the CONSUMER applies bf16 rounding where reference does). My xw trick: y = rstd * Σ (x*normw) * W. The DIFFERENCE: bf16 rounding of the INPUT per-element vs exact-then-scaled. Error per element ~ 0.4% relative on the INPUTS — since GEMV output is a sum of 2304 independent terms, output relative error is ~ 0.4%/sqrt(2304)... but WAIT no: bf16(x_norm) has per-element rounding: xn = xw*rstd*(1+e_k), e_k ~ 0.4%. Completely fine vs 0.98 cosine. But here's the thing: the ROUTER top-k — logit diffs ~1e-4 relative → flips only on knife-edge ties (probability tiny). Same as before. OK proceed. One more correctness check — attention self-inclusion: cache row L written in P1 (rope+append) ✓ P2 covers L+1 positions ✓. ### MoE P3 details (gate/up, dynamic counter + router-race): tiles layout: [router k-split ×4 (warp-items hmm make CTA-items: 4)] + [shared gu: 1024/128 = 8] + [seed moe_acc: 9 warp-items... make 2 CTA items (1152 each)] + [routed gu: 8×8 = 64] Router tile: 64 logits × K-chunk (576): lane parallelism: 512 thr: warp w: logits cols... per tile: K=576 rows=288 packed... this phase's xn K=2304. tile k-chunk = 576 k → 288 rows: lane-col mapping: 64 cols / router weight (64,2304): bf16 not int4!! router weight = AB bf16 plain GEMV: per k: 64 weight elems (128B) × x_n[k]: warp: lanes split 64 (2 per lane): fp32 acc×2 → partial → 4 k-chunks → atomicAdd to logit[64]... after finishing its 4 chunks?? dynamic + arrival counting: EACH CTA-item atomically adds logits partial + increments done-counter (release semantics); CTA seeing done==4 (last) computes softmax+topk+w → writes ids/w → sets READY flag (release semantics via atomicExch + __threadfence). Other CTAs: routed-gu items spin on READY (volatile poll). logits zeroed?? — zero them in a previous phase (P1: logits=0 needs write-before... put logits zeroing into the "seed moe_acc" items?? those are IN P3 — same-phase hazard for ROUTER items: router items atomicAdd — must be zero BEFORE first add — any router item could run first thing... So zero logits in P1 (or block P0) — safe: logits last read at previous block's router → zeroing in current block P0 fine ✓ (KDA P1 does... let me place: KDA P0 extra item zeroes logits + the READY flag reset?? flag monotonic-gen trick avoids reset: READY value = gen·10 + block_id (monotonic!) ✓ no reset needed ✓ just zero logits (and ready flag doesn't need reset). ✓ KDA P0 gets a "side work" item; MLA P1 too... per block: logits zero during block P0 ✓. fastest-path top-k: single warp: 64 fp32: lane partial max... 8 rounds argmax with -inf masking: ~8×(shuffle reduce argmax 5-step) — trivial. w = probs: normalize: wsum; w8 = p/sum × 2.446 ✓ fp32 → write ids int32 + w8 fp32 → threadfence → flag=READY. Routed gu item (e-slot j, coltile): EXPERT ID = ids[j] read post-flag ✓ weight reads gate/up slice for expert e. ### P4 down (atomic adds into moe_acc): MOE_ACC seeded with h_acc (P3 items ✓). tiles: (expert-slot 9 × coltile 2304/128=18) = 162 items: per item: full-K GEMV (K=1024 → 512 rows) × 128 cols: warp k-split ×16 (512/16=32 rows per warp → HALF group!! K=1024: groups=8 → warp w takes group w (w<8) → warps 8..15 idle?!). Chunking by groups: K=1024 → 8 groups over 16 warps → half idle → down phase ~2× slow?? tile = (slot, 128c): per-tile bytes = 128cols×512bytes = 64KB; 162 tiles × 64KB = 10.6MB ✓ with half-warps idle: DRAM feed from 162 CTA × 8 warps... ~ still fine?? per SM: 8 warps × 32 × 4B × ~8 = 8KB in flight — short. ALTERNATIVE: CTA-item = (slot, 64c) → 324 items: same bytes, util ~ same... Balance: down-phase ~ 10.6MB / (8/16 util × 1.7TB/s...) hmm ~ 13µs? that's slow vs ideal 6µs. IMPROVE: warp handles HALF-group units (32 rows): s/z unchanged within half (s per group — sub-group ✓ just reuse group scalars); warps 0..15 take 16 half-group units = 512 rows ✓ full occupancy: warp w: rows w*32..w*32+32 (unit = half-group: s/z from group w/2 ✓). GENERALIZE k-unit = 32 ROWS (64 k) everywhere (finer) with group = unit/2... for K=2304: K2=1152 rows @32 = 36 units / 16 warps ✓ better balance everywhere. Choose K-UNIT = 32 rader than 64: s/z apply per GROUP: accumulate HALF-group dot but apply s,z only at group end (two units) → partial z-term: y_c += s_c·(dot − z·xsum): apply per-unit with z-term needing unit's xsum (64 elems): fine: correction per unit ✓ (algebra identical: Σ over group = Σ over halves). ✓ K-UNIT=32 rows. Hmm but k-unit smaller → more smem-reduce overhead per tile; trade ok. ### P1 S-tile k_c staging: smem: k_c[128], v_c... q_c: 3×128 fp32 + gexp 128 = 2KB ✓ ### Now — attention smem sizing: q_abs smem 32×512 fp32 = 64KB + q_rope smem 32×64 = 8KB + c-stage: 32 pos × 576 bf16 = 36KB (+double-buffer v2) + m/l 0.3KB + misc = ~110KB → occ=1 CTA/SM (max dyn smem 227K on H100 ✓ cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize set to ~200KB ✓). BUT dynamic smem sized at LAUNCH for the WHOLE kernel = max across phases: 64+8+36+8(reduce)+... ~ 116KB → occ = 1 ✓ G = 114. (Attention only engages at MLA anyway; other phases waste the smem allocation — fine.) ### Compute budget for attention per CTA-tile (ch=257, 32 heads): per pos: 32h×(512+64) dots = 18.4K MAC → per tile 257× = 4.7M MAC → fp32 FMA + smem loads ~ (earlier est 128cy/pos vs DRAM 155B/cy-per-SM... full-SM DRAM ≈ 1.7TB/114 = 15GB/s/SM = 7.4B/cy@2.05GHz → per pos 1.15KB → 155cy) ~ roughly balanced ✓ attn @16k ≈ (19MB / 1.7TB/s) × ~1.15 ≈ 13µs ✓. ### MISC: exp/log: use __expf/__logf? use expf (accurate): 4096 gexp per tile-redundant... P1 gexp: exp per tile (128 vals computed redundantly 4× per head... use __expf fast-math? -use_fast_math affects ALL — softmax/rope precision: fp32 expf needed for rope (large args 16k rad — __expf fine for exp... rope needs SINF/COSF accurate for |x| up to 16384 — __sinf does range reduction OK-ish (2^-21 abs err... at 16k rad, fp32 sin arg reduction error could be ~1e-3 relative — acceptable? torch cos fp32 (accurate ~1ulp). __sinf error for large args: documented max abs err grows... Use sinf/cosf (accurate versions: SLOW ~ 20-40 cy each — rope: 32×64 + 64 values per step = 2112 trig per LAYER-step at ONE warp redundancy... ~ 2112×30cy = 63K cy per warp — spread over 4 warps... ~ 30-60µs?!?! NO WAIT — per warp doing rope tile: warp items: q_rope 2048 elems: 16 warps → 128/lane × 30cy = 3840cy ≈ 2µs ✓ FINE. And not per-tile redundant. Precompute cos/sin table??? pos varies per step: PRECOMPUTE ONCE at prepare for ALL positions up to Lmax (16512 × 32 floats cos + sin: inv[32] × pos: 1MB fp32 — precompute at prepare via torch! ✓✓ Then rope phase just LOADS cos[pos, i], sin[pos,i] (32 bf... fp32 each) per position — trivial + EXACT match with... wait reference computes them in fp32 per call identically: `theta**(arange/dim)` then pos*inv, cos, sin fp32 — deterministic → precomputed table with SAME torch ops = BIT-IDENTICAL ✓✓✓ DO THAT. So in prepare: `inv = 1/(theta**(arange(0,64,2)/64))` fp32; `pos = arange(Lmax)`; ang = outer; COSC/SINC tables (Lmax,32) fp32 → device — 4.2MB ✓. Kernel rope loads COSC[pos][i] ✓. ### Kernel arg struct complete: ``` struct Call { const bf16* x_in; bf16* x_out; float* S[3]; bf16* wnd[9]; // cq0,ck0,cv0,cq1,... int L, fresh, nchunk, ch; long long gen; }; ``` Static tensors (WB,SB,AB,SC,BAR,ws_ckv,ws_kr,cosc,sinc,COSC) → `__constant__` struct `Stat` — cudaMemcpyToSymbol at prepare ✓ (or just more Call fields — constant is cleaner). Let me get concrete about the gemv CTA function (workhorse): ```cpp // One CTA-tile: 128 cols of a quant matrix, k-split over 16 warps. // xn in smem (fp32), K rows K2 = K/2 packed. // wbase: byte offset into WB; sbase/zbase: elem offsets into SB (bf16, stride N) // y: fp32 out (or atomic add), scale rstd (1 if none) template <bool ATOMIC> __device__ void gemv_tile(const Dev& D, long long wbase, long long sbase, int N, int K, const float* xn /*smem*/, float rstd, float* y /*global, 128 cols*/) { // lane t covers cols 4t..4t+3 (t in 0..31) // warp w covers k-units of 32 rows: units = K2/32; unit u = w, w+16, ... int wid = threadIdx.x >> 5, lane = threadIdx.x & 31; int K2 = K>>1; int units = K2 >> 5; // 32-row units (K multiple of 64 k = 32 rows) const uint8_t* wp = D.WB + wbase; float dot[4] = {0,0,0,0}; for (int u = wid; u < units; u += 16) { int r0 = u*32; // first packed row of unit // groups in unit: unit covers 64 k = HALF group; group g = r0/64 → (u/2) const bf16* sp = D.SB + sbase + (u>>1)*N + ... hmm sbase already includes group row stride... store scales as (G, N) rows: sp = sbase + g*N ... } } ``` The 16-warp k-split leads: warps 0..15 × units: K=2304: units = 36 → warps get 2-3 units. Per unit: rows r0..r0+31 (packed); per row: xn[2r], xn[2r+1] broadcast; w32 = row's uint32 at col 4·lane... byte offset: r*N + col0 (col0 = tile_col*128 + lane*4). Inner loop unroll 8 rows: ``` #pragma unroll for (int i = 0; i < 32; i += 8) { // 8 rows unrolled uint32_t wv[8]; for (int j = 0; j < 8; j++) wv[j] = *(const uint32_t*)(wp + (r0+i+j)*N + c0); for j...: per byte b of wv[j]: lohi magic... } ``` compiler will handle. Per byte: w_lo → xn[2r] product; accumulate into dot[b]? WAIT — byte index b within uint32 = COLUMN offset (cols c0, c0+1, c0+2, c0+3): dot[b] += wval_lo·xn_lo[r] + wval_hi·xn_hi[r] — per uint32: 8 FMA (+corrections)... Per unit (32 rows = 64 k): xsum needs Σ xn over the UNIT's 64 values: lane accumulates from broadcast loads: 64 adds... hmm 64 adds per 32 rows × 8 FMA/row = 256 FMA — xsum adds = 25% OVERHEAD on fp pipe. Alternative: PRECOMPUTE unit xsums in... each warp its own slice → cross-warp redundancy... OR change factorization: y = Σ_g s·Σw·xn − s·z·Σxn = Σ_g [s·dot_g] − [s·z·xsum_g]: precompute s·z·xsum_g per group ONCE per (matrix, col-tile) — but shared across warps... each warp could compute partial... no no — simpler: precompute xsum PER GROUP globally ONCE per x-version! It's (K/128) values Σ xn: computed during xn STAGING (cooperative: 512 thr × per-thread partials → but block-reduce per group (≤32 groups)... staging loop: thread sums its elements per group → atomic to smem per group?? 32 groups × ... simple: after xn staged, 32 groups × 128 values: assign WARP per group: lane partial + shuffle → smem xsum[g] ✓ ONE pass ~ fast. THEN inner loop has NO xsum overhead ✓ (z-term at unit end: y_c += s_c·dot_c − sz_c·xsum_g — need s_c,z_c: 4 cols: lane loads s (uint64 = 4 bf16) ✓. Wait — actually z-term placement: with xsum_g precomputed: at UNIT end (half-group), this warp has dot_c over HALF the group: apply: y_c += s_c·dot_c − (s_c·z_c)·hsum_u where hsum_u = xsum over THIS half (precomputed per GROUP not per half!)... make precomp per 64-k (half-group): xsumH[u]: units ≤ 64 values — stage computes per-unit sums (same mechanism: 64 values per lane?? assign warps to units: lane sums 2 → shuffle) ✓ xsums per 32-row unit. correction per unit: y_c += s_c·(dot_c − z_c·xsumH_u) — s_c from group u>>1 ✓ all good. Even better: should corrections go through per-unit? Per unit per lane-col: 1 fma + 1 mul + s/z loads (16B) — vs 256 FMA inner — ~3% ✓. Or layout [w][128...]: whatever, it's tiny traffic. BAR (ull): [0..31] arrive, [32..63] release, [64..71] router-flags (per block: ready-gen), [72..135] work counters (64), [136..143] router-done counters (per block) — pre-zeroed at prepare; work counters re-zeroed in P8 ✓; router-done counters monotonic-trick: reset at P0-item (zero them) ✓ or monotonic: done-target = gen*4+4: zero in P0-item (safe: P0 before P3 ✓). Cooperative launch + custom barrier at ~1.1µs confirmed. Now I have the full architecture:

  • CUDA C++ megakernel via load_inline, cooperative launch (guaranteed co-residency), custom 2-word monotonic barriers (~23/launch)
  • GEMV: warp-per-column-group with uint32 loads, magic-bias 0x4B000000|nibble unpack (exact integer fp32), s/z folded per 64-k unit with precomputed x-sums
  • All x inputs staged to smem with exact bf16 rounding (matching reference's rounding points), rstd factored out
  • MLA absorb (attention in latent space), dynamic atomic work-counters for load balance

Let me start building the full solution. First the Python module tree + layout.

--- /dev/null
+++ b//ephemeral/mega-outputs/runs/20260716_091712_kinetic-claude_kinetic-0715_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/solution.py
@@ -0,0 +1,577 @@
+"""Kimi-Linear W4A16 hybrid decode unit - single-launch CUDA megakernel solution.
+
+The whole per-token forward (4 blocks: KDA,KDA,KDA,MLA; each attn + 64-expert MoE,
+int4 fused dequant GEMVs, conv, recurrent state update, latent-cache attention,
+router+topk, RMSNorms, residuals) is fused into ONE CUDA __global__ kernel launched
+ cooperatively once per step() with grid-wide software barriers between phases.
+
+Weights are repacked once (prepare, off the timed path) into flat blobs:
+  WB: all packed int4 weights (uint8, (in//2, out) tiles concatenated)
+  SB: all scales+zeros (bf16, per weight: [scales(G,N)][zeros(G,N)])
+  AB: aux bf16 weights (norms, router, beta, conv)
+The decode streams int4 bytes once; dequant uses the exact-integer magic bias
+(0x4B000000|nibble -> fp32) so no dequantized bf16 matrix is ever materialized.
… +564 more lines
tool

reasoning
Now the big piece: mega_impl.py containing the CUDA source and the extension build. This is where all the careful kernel work goes. Let me write it methodically. Kernel structure, let me re-derive the phases and their tile tables precisely, with slot/barrier numbering: Threads per block: THR = 512 (16 warps). Grid G = runtime. Work item distribution: per phase a work counter ctr (index into BAR ull area at BAR_WORK). CTA does: ``` for(;;){ __shared__ int t; if(tid==0) t = atomicAdd(&ctr,1); __syncthreads(); int tt = t; __syncthreads(); if(tt >= NT) break; do_tile(tt); } ``` Need `t` in smem: I'll use a small static __shared__ int (separate from dynamic smem) — careful with syncthreads reuse. Dynamic smem layout (extern __shared__ char smem[]): - xn stage: up to 4096 fp32 at offset 0 - xsumH: up to 128 fp32 (after xn? overlapping? needed concurrently with xn! xn 16KB + xsum 512B) - reduce buf for gemv: 16 warps × 128 cols fp32 = 8KB - smem tv (work counter broadcast): int - attention area: q_abs bf16 (32×512 = 32KB), q_rope bf16 (32×64=4KB), c-stage bf16: 16 pos × 576 = 18KB ×2(double) = 36KB, m/l/acc-info..., overalp with xn/reduce via union: attention doesn't coexist with gemv staging ✓. - For the gu-phase silu etc... no extra. - S-phase smem: q_c,k_c,v_c,gexp: 4×128 fp32 = 2KB + red 8KB (not needed)... plus xn staging area. - qabs-item staging: qs[128] fp32 + zq scalar: 512B + red... gemv reduce buf used as partial storage for qabs? qabs writes scalar per lane... uses different sink (see below). Max dynamic = attention: 32+4+36+ small = ~74KB + margin. Set 96KB dyn smem. Hmm at 512 threads with ~100+ regs... only 1 CTA/SM either way. Could raise smem to 120KB doesn't matter. Actually for safety of occupancy let me put q_abs as fp16/bf16... fine bf16 32KB. Let me now carefully write each phase's device code. ### Common device helpers ```cpp using bf16 = __nv_bfloat16; struct Dev { const uint8_t* WB; const bf16* SB; const bf16* AB; float* SC; unsigned long long* BAR; bf16* WSC; bf16* WSK; const float* COSC; const float* SINC; bf16* XOUT; const long long* LAYV; }; struct Call { const bf16* x_in; float* S[3]; const bf16* wnd[9]; const bf16* ckv; const bf16* krc; int L; int fresh; int nchunk; int ch; long long gen; }; __device__ Dev DEV; // set via setup() copying to device global? Actually simpler: pass Dev by value as kernel param along with Call. ``` Simplest: kernel params: `__global__ void mega(Dev d, Call c)`. Host keeps Dev static filled at setup. LAYV index mapping: python names list `self._lay_names` in fixed order — C++ reads offsets by INDEX. I'll define an enum in C++ matching the python-generated order BUT python order is dict-iteration order of lay.wb then sb then ab: wb order = insertion: b0.q,b0.k,b0.v,b0.g,b0.o,b0.eg,b0.eu,b0.ed,b0.sg,b0.su,b0.sd, b1.q... b3.q,b3.kva,b3.kvb,b3.o,b3.eg... then sb same, ab: b0.an,b0.mn,b0.rt,b0.beta,b0.conv, b1..., b3.an,b3.mn,b3.rt. Rather than hardcoding indices (fragile), python writes names into a file?? No — cleaner: python passes offsets as a NAMED set of scalar args... 40 int64 args = pybind marshalling heavy. Alternative: python builds the int64 tensor and the C++ enum is generated from a PYTHON-COMPUTED ordering I fix via a sorted canonical order. Let me define canonical order explicitly: C++ side computes its own expected layout (constexpr order: same as Lay)! Because the layout was defined by me — C++ can recompute it. But scales/zeros for experts etc... it's the same arithmetic I've already expressed in Lay... duplicating in C++ = error risk again. DECISION: pass ONE int64 pointer (LAYV); the index→meaning map fixed by construction in `Lay` AND mirrored in a C++ enum written BY HAND carefully. Debug will catch mismatches (offsets validated: python prints expected offsets, kernel debug mode can print too — meh, careful review it is). Actually SIMPLEST BULLETPROOF: python computes offsets as a python dict; extension `setup()` receives individual named scalar args in a FIXED ORDER listed in the pybind signature... ~40 int64 args once at setup (not per step) → fine! setup(wb, sb, ab, ..., wb_b0_q, wb_b0_k, ...) — verbose but zero index-map risk. ~40 args in one function def — OK let me do that via **kwargs? pybind needs explicit. I'll take a middle path: setup takes the offset int64 TENSOR + C++ reads via an INDEX ENUM that I generate... ugh. OK do explicit list arg as tuple of int64 => vector<int64_t>: `setup(..., std::vector<int64_t> offs)`; python passes list in the CANONICAL order matching C++ enum (documented). Acceptable given careful documentation both sides. Canonical order (C++ enum): for bi in 0..3: for p in pieces(bi): W_off, S_off — and A offs at end: enum: idx = 2*(bi*11 + pi) for wb piece pi... Let me just define ONCE in python: `PIECES(bi)` returns list of 11 wb tags + 11 sb tags (same names!) + ab tags (3 or 5). The off vector = [wb[tag] for tags in order] + [sb[tag]] + [ab[tag]]. Document order in code. ### barrier / work functions ```cpp __device__ __forceinline__ void gsync(Dev& D, int slot, unsigned long long tgt) { __syncthreads(); if (threadIdx.x == 0) { unsigned long long* arr = D.BAR + BAR_ARR + slot; unsigned long long old = atomicAdd(arr, 1ULL); if (old == tgt - 1) atomicExch(D.BAR + BAR_REL + slot, tgt); volatile unsigned long long* r = D.BAR + BAR_REL + slot; unsigned long long v = *r; while (v < tgt) v = *r; } __syncthreads(); } ``` tgt = (gen+1)*G computed per launch. slot counter local. Work: ```cpp __device__ __forceinline__ int next_work(Dev& D, int cid, int* sm_t) { if (threadIdx.x == 0) *sm_t = (int)atomicAdd(D.BAR + BAR_WORK + cid, 1ULL); __syncthreads(); return *sm_t; } ``` Each call site: `while (true) { int t = next_work(...); if (t >= NT) break; tile(t); }` — syncthreads INSIDE loop ok (convergent). ### rstd staging x versions: - XIN: x_in (bf16 ptr) — block 0 attn - XA: sc_moacc (fp32, round per-element) — blocks 1..3 attn - XH: sc_hacc (fp32) — moe phase ```cpp // stage normed x: smem xn[HID] fp32 = bf16(bf16(x_k) * rstd * nrm_k), returns rstd template <int XSRC> __device__ float stage_xn(float* xn, const bf16* x_in, const float* x_f, const bf16* nrm, float* sm_red); ``` Implementation: partial sums per thread (4-5 elems), reduce via smem (16 warp partials + second round), rstd; second pass write xn. Both passes strided over 2304 by 512 threads. Cooperative sum: thread t handles elems t, t+512, t+1024, t+1536, t+2048: 5 loads (last partial masked: only threads < 256... 2304 = 4*512 + 256: thread t<256 does 5th). Sum = Σ x² (fp32; x bf16 cvt or fp32-round-bf16). Block reduce: warp shuffles + smem 16 slots + warp0 reduce + smem broadcast: standard. ### GEMV tile (quant matrix) ```cpp // computes y[col0..col0+127] for quant weight at (wbase bytes, sbase elems) of shape (K2 rows, N cols) // xn: smem staged fp32 (K), xsu: smem per-32-row-unit sums of xn // MODE 0: store fp32 (y = rstd*val) MODE 1: store fp32 residual (y = res[col] + rstd*val, res fp32 ptr) // MODE 2: atomicAdd fp32 (y += wgt*rstd*val) -- rstd=1 for moe paths // MODE 3: silu gate path (separate function) __device__ void gemv_tile(const Dev& D, bf16 x? ...) ``` Wait — s/z: SB stores scales then zeros: sbase = sb off, zbase = sb off + (K/128)*N. Per unit u (32 packed rows = 64 k), columns c0..c0+3 (lane l): ``` // per unit: rows r = u*32 + i for i in 0..31 // s,z for group g=u/2 at col c: load once per unit pair?? per unit we apply HALF-group correction: // y_c += s_c*(dot_c - z_c * xsum_u) -- where dot_c over unit's 64k, xsum_u from smem ``` Hmm but s,z loads per unit: 16B — fine. Careful: dot includes NO scale; accumulate dot per unit separately: ``` float dot[4] = {0..}; for (i = 0; i < 32; i++) { int r = u*32 + i; uint32_t wv = *(const uint32_t*)(D.WB + wbase + (size_t)r*N + c0); float xl = xn[2*r], xh = xn[2*r+1]; // bytes b = 0..3: nibble lo = k=2r, hi = k=2r+1 #pragma unroll for (int b = 0; b < 4; b++) { uint32_t byte = (wv >> (8*b)) & 0xFF; float wlo = magic(byte & 0xF); float whi = magic(byte >> 4); dot[b] += wlo*xl + whi*xh; } } // fold int g = u >> 1; float xs = xsu[u]; uint2 s4 = *(const uint2*)(D.SB + sbase + (size_t)g*N + c0); // 4 bf16 = 8B uint2 z4 = *(const uint2*)(D.SB + zbase + (size_t)g*N + c0); for b: float s = f32(((bf16*)&s4)[b]), z = f32(((bf16*)&z4)[b]); y[b] += s*(dot[b] - z*xs); // s*(dot) - (s*z)*xs: y[b] = fmaf(s, dot[b] - z*xs, y[b]) ``` magic: `__int_as_float(0x4B000000 | n) - 8388608.0f` — exact: b&0xF in [0,15]: (0x4B000000|n) as float = 8388608+n exact, minus 2^23 → n exact. ✓ Wait — one subtlety: my uint32 read of `byte = (wv >> 8b) & 0xFF`: b indexes BYTE = column c0+b ✓ each byte: low nibble = k=2r (EVEN row FIRST = packed row r covers k=2r and 2r+1 with lo=2r ✓ since w_packed[k/2] = w[2k2...(low = even k)] ✓. Loads: `wv` uint32 per row: rows unrolled → compiler will emit multiple LD.32; good enough v1 (or uint4 later: 16B = 4 rows?? NO — uint4 would span 16 cols along the row! (contiguous bytes along N). per-lane uint32 = 4 cols is the coalesced optimum at 128B/warp. PER-LANE wider load = wider warp transaction: uint2 per lane (8B = 8 cols): warp = 256B per row... 128-byte lines fully utilized ✓ uint2 = fewer instructions. But cols per lane 8 → acc regs 8... Let me start uint32/4cols; optimize later.) Inner-loop x broadcasts: `xn[2*r]` from smem: 32 loads each — compiler may hoist?? it must load per r (different addresses)... LDS per row ×2 — 64 LDS per 32 rows per col-load... LDS.throughput limit: 128B/cy/SM shared... per warp per row: 2 broadcast LDS (4B) — broadcast = 1 bank cycle each: 2 cy/row/warp × 16 warps / ... per row per CTA: 16 warps issue 2 LDS each = 32 cy of LSU per 32-row units... meh parallel with DRAM. ALTERNATIVE: keep xn in REGISTERS?? warp's k-chunk fixed → but dynamic tile: k-slice per warp determined at tile time: weights: warp loops units u = wid*? hmm — contiguous warp k-ranges (warp w takes rows w*(K2/16)...): xn slice per warp constant across tiles (same phase) → could preload x-slice into registers ONCE PER PHASE (each warp 1152/16 = 72 rows → 144 floats = 144 regs?! too many). NO — smem broadcast fine. Now — pipelining: per row load latency ~500ns DRAM... warp issues per unit: 32 sequential uint32 loads with unroll(8?) → ~8 outstanding → per warp in-flight 32B... 16 warps × 114 = 1824 warps × 32B = 58KB in flight?!?! WAY TOO LITTLE (need ~1.4MB). Disagreement with earlier estimate: earlier I said per-thread 8 × 4B = 32B in flight. 29200 threads × 32B = 930KB... wait 1824 warps × 32 lanes = 58368 threads?? NO — G=114 CTA × 512 = 58368 threads = 1824 warps ✓ each warp 32 lanes × 1 load each = 32 loads of 4B outstanding per unroll-step?? Per unroll step of 8: each LANE has 8 uint32 loads queued → per warp 8×32×4B = 1KB in flight → 1824 KB total ✓ 1.8MB ✓ FINE (I miscounted — per lane 8 loads of 4B = 32B; 58K threads × 32B = 1.8MB ✓). To get 8 outstanding per lane: unroll 8 rows. The s/z/apply overhead per unit is small. Unroll the ROW loop inside unit by 8 (32 rows = 4 chunks of 8): compiler unrolls... write `#pragma unroll 8`?? For `for(i=0;i<32;i++)` with all independent — compiler (nvcc -O3) will unroll fully and schedule; trust + verify with ncu later. Sink modes: partials to smem red[warp][col] fp32 — wait store `y[4]` per lane = 4 floats → red[warp][4*lane+b] (+ bank conflict but small traffic)... syncthreads → final: 128 cols / 16 warps: warp takes 8 cols, lane j<8 reduces 16 × adds rstd + residual... Actually simpler final: EACH warp handles 8 cols: lane assignment: 8 cols over 32 lanes → 4 lanes per col?? just have lane j (j<8) per col: reads red[0..15][col] (16 loads, broadcast patterns ok) → sum → store mode ops. Low cost either way. ### gate/up silu tile Same loop structure but TWO matrices (gate then up — or fused loop: per row read gate uint32 and up uint32 (2 streams) → dot_g[4], dot_u[4], apply scales at unit ends (both), silu at sink: h = silu(rstd·g)·(rstd·u) → store MOEH fp32. silu = g*sigmoid(g) = g/(1+expf(-g)). NOTE reference applies silu to the fp32 GEMV out — our rstd·g fp32 ✓ no bf16 rounding (ref: x.float() @ W — the GEMV inputs are bf16-rounded (xn ✓) outputs fp32 ✓). ### down tile: MODE=2 atomicAdd with wgt: y[col] += wgt · val (rstd=1). Sink = atomicAdd. Reads x = MOEH (fp32, staged smem per item: 1024 elems = cheap) — need xsu for moe_h (per-unit sums over 64k → 16 units: computed post-stage cooperatively (16 units × warp-per... simple: during stage, each thread's loaded elems... I'll do a second mini-pass: for u in (wid): sum 64 elems → warp reduce → smem xsu[u]... ### KDA P1 conv+S tile (h, dvchunk) smem: qc,kc,vc,gc(exp) 128 fp32 each (2KB). - stage: 512 thr → each thread computes channels?? per warp-tile... this tile is a WHOLE CTA? tile (h,4chunks) — work granularity CTA-item = (h) with warps splitting dv?? NO — CTA-item (h, dvquad of 32): 128 items ✓. Per item: stage conv for channels h*128..+127 (all 512 threads: 128 channels / 512 → thread computes iff tid<128... or 128 lanes×... simpler: first 128 threads compute one channel each): for cv in (q,k,v): win rows: wnd[?]: these are state pointers (3, C) each: rows r0..2, cols c = h*128+t (t<128); val = raw[c] (SC_QKVG + cv*4096 + c) → round bf16; compute out = silu(Σ wnd[j][c]·convw[cv][c][j]) → store smem q/k/v_c[t]; scale q by 1/sqrt(128) → WAIT scale: ref multiplies AFTER silu-round...: q = conv_out.float() * Dk^-0.5: our qc fp32 = float(round(silu))·scale ✓ k,v = float(round(silu)) ✓. gexp: thread t: g_raw[c] → bf16 round → float → gexp = expf(-softplus) → smem gc[t]. beta: β[h] = sigmoid(bf16(Σ xn·beta_w[·,h])) — needs xn and rstd of the BLOCK's attn-x: xn staged per CTA at P0-phase-start (x-version same) — but P1 is a NEW phase: stage again per CTA (cheap) — with NORM = attn_norm ✓ (beta and conv are functions of normed input ✓). Per item: 2304-sum: all 512 threads cumulatively: t-strided loads xn·beta_w[t*...+h]: partial → block reduce → β scalar (smem bc): weight reads: beta col h: stride 2304·2B — scattered 2B loads ✗ slow-ish: 2304 loads × 128 items = 295K strided 2B loads from L2 (59 K sectors × ... each 2B load = 32B sector → 9.4MB effective — hmm 5µs at worst!! Alternative: precompute beta ONCE per block... in P1-head-items?? all items need it... PRE-STAGE beta[32] globally in P0's side-item (one warp: 2304×32 = 73K MAC — one warp = 2304 lanes... 32 lanes × 2304 MAC each = ~2-3µs at P0-tail... hidden under P0 streaming ✓) → smem/scratch SC: beta read per item = 1 broadcast ✓✓ BETTER: add beta-precompute to the P0 side-item (it also zeroes logits — warp does both). P0 side-item: warp0: β for 32 heads + logits zero. β scratch = SC? add SC_BETA 32 fp32 (alias somewhere — put at SC_XSUM+64). Let me add SC_BETA. Hmm wait — P0 side-item is executed by ONE CTA; OTHER CTAs' P1 items read beta AFTER the P0→P1 barrier ✓ safe. - S update: warps split (dk? dv?): lanes own dv: item covers dv 32 (dv0 = chunk*32): warps: dk split: 128 dk / 16 warps = 8 dk per warp: warp w rows dk = w*8 .. +7: each reads S row (32 floats at (h, dk, dv0..): coalesced ✓), computes: S·gexp[dk] → pred accumulate?? pred[dv] = Σ_dk S'[dk,dv]·k[dk] — cross-dk reduction ACROSS warps!! order: (1) all warps compute S' store... restructure: pred needs sum over all dk BEFORE S update. (b) pred[dv]: Σ over all 128 dk — cross-warp: partial per warp: lane: Σ its 8 (dot with kc[dk] from smem) → partial[8 per lane...]: warp-partials → smem [16][32] → reduce by ... second pass: read lanes sum 16 → pred[dv-lane] hmm which warp... ALL warps need pred (each updates its rows) → pred must be BROADCAST to all warps: compute into smem pred[32] via a reduction: (i) each warp writes its partial[32] (lane-wise) to red2[w][32] → sync → (ii) warp 0: pred[d] = Σ_w red2[w][d] → smem → sync. (iii) all warps: S_new[dk,dv] = S' + β·kc[dk]·(vc[dv] − pred[dv]) → write GLOBAL S + accumulate o[dv] += S_new·qc[dk] partial → red2 reuse → warp0 reduce → o[dv0..31] store (bf16-rounded to SC_O bf16? xproj input reads bf16 ✓ store as bf16 SC_O... wait SC layout said O fp32 — change O to... reference rounds o to bf16 ✓ store bf16 (SC area is fp32 tensor — store bf16 bit patterns?? type-punning a float* as bf16* — I'll add a separate small bf16 scratch tensor or just store fp32-rounded values float(bf16(x)) ✓ keep simple: float(bf16) into SC_O fp32 ✓ SAME shape; P2 oproj stage: reads SC_O fp32 → xn exactly those values ✓ inputs match bf16 ref exactly ✓. For block0: hmm x = x_in bf16: res loads must read bf16 → variant flag (block index passed: residual source selection by block!) — gemv sink: if blk==0 read x_in bf16 cvt else moacc fp32 ✓ runtime branch per tile (col-constant branch cheap). window writeback items: 12 items (3 tensors × 4 colblocks of 1024): shift: for c in colblock: t2 = wnd[2][c], t1...: new: row0 <- old row1, row1 <- old row2, row2 <- val (from raw scratch: SC_QKVG + qi*4096 + c rounded bf16). Per item 1024 channels: warp-cooperative: lanes stride. CAREFUL: in-place read-write SAME warp-slice (lane reads its c's triple then writes ✓ register-temporary ✓ no cross-lane hazard ✓). ### P3 MoE gate/up Items: 0..3 router kchunks; 4..11 shared gu (8 coltiles); 12,13 seed moe_acc (2304 / 2×1152); 14..77 routed gu. Router kchunk item (4 of them): cols 64 logits over kchunk = 576 (of 2304): warp-split: 64 cols / 16 warps → warp = 4 cols: lanes=4 lanes?? per warp 4 cols over 32 lanes: k-split INSIDE warp: lane handles 576/32 = 18 k... xn smem (full 2304! staged this phase ✓). Implementation: per warp col c (4 of them): per lane: partial = Σ_{k = 18·lane..} xn[k]·rt[c][k] — rt is AB bf16 (64,2304): element [c][k]: loads 18 bf16 strided?? k-contig within row c: 18 consecutive bf16 = 36B: per warp per col: lanes read DIFFERENT k-chunks of row c — stride 36B — L1 sectors: fine. partial → shuffle reduce → lane0 atomicAdd(logit + c, rstd·partial)?? — the rstd multiply: apply once... apply at atomicAdd time by the reducer: but then Σ over kchunks each scaled by rstd ✓ fine. after item completes its atomicAdds: `old = atomicAdd(&RDONE[blk], 1); if (old == 3) { topk code; }》 — one thread does the counter; the LAST item's CTA does topk+weights+flag (all by thread... one warp better: warp0 of that CTA: softmax: compute max & sum over 64 (logits fp32 in scratch → p_i: 2 lanes... 64 elems: warp lanes 2 each: max-shuffle-reduce, exp, sum, normalize by (sum+1e-9)... wait softmax FIRST softmax then topk over probs ✓ top-8: each lane j<8: scan 64 for argmax of remaining (mask) → 8×(64-scan+reduce)... ~8×2×32 shuffle-cy ≈ trivial. weights w8[j] = p/sum8... WAIT: ref: w = topk probs; w = w/(Σw + 1e-9)×2.446 — normalize over the TOP-8 sum ✓. write SC_W8 + SC_IDS + __threadfence(); atomicExch(&RFLAG[blk], gen). Routed-gu items: wait: `while(volatile RFLAG[blk] < gen) ;` then read ids — ids[7-j...] item 14+j*8+ct: expert = ids[j] ✓ weight slice base = eg + expert·(1152·1024) ... note experts' gate weights flattened ex-major: (64, 1152, 1024) ✓. Shared gu items: expert 0 of sg/su (offsets direct). Seed items: moacc[c] = hacc[c] (1152 elems each). ALL non-router items must SPIN on flag before reading... shared/seed don't need flag!! only routed-gu items spin ✓ (order: CTAs likely grab router+shared first ✓). ### P4 down: 162 items: item = slot·18 + ct: slot<8 → expert ids[slot], wgt = w8[slot]; slot 8 → shared sd, wgt 1. GEMV MODE atomic: moacc[col] += wgt·val. x-source: MOEH[slot·1024 ...] staged (4KB/item). NOTE gemv needs xstaged per ITEM (moe_h slices) ✓. ### Attention phases (MLA block = b3) P0: 48 q-tiles + 5 kva-tiles + 1 side: q tiles → SC_MLAQ (rstd·); kva → SC_KV (fp32... values rounded bf16 at P1 use ✓). Note kva N=576: coltiles of 128: 4.5: tile 4 covers cols 512..639 → OUT OF BOUNDS rows!! weight rows are 576B → reading cols ≥576 reads NEXT row ✓ discussed — blob ends padded 1KB ✓ kva is NOT last in blob ✓ ok. y cols ≥576 → SC_KV sized 640 ✓. P1: qabs items = (h, jc) 256 + rope-q 4 + kvrow 1 + fresh·L copy. qabs item (h, jc): jc = j2-chunk of 32 packed rows: qs stage: s,g fixed: g = jc>>1; qs[d] = qnope[h][d]·s[g][h·256+d]: 512thr compute 128 vals; zq = Σ_d z[g][h·256+d]·qs[d]... wait zq = Σ_d z_d·s_d·q_d = Σ_d z_d·qs_d ✓ block-reduce over 128 → smem scalar. lanes = rows: 32 rows of the chunk (j2 = jc·32 + lane): each lane: reads its row (128B at (j2, h*256)) sequentially: 32 uint32: per byte: lo→dot_lo += w_lo·qs[d], hi→dot_hi += qs[d]: per byte 2 FMA × w... wait per byte: w_lo · qs[4·b?? no: COLS d: byte b covers d = h*256 + 4b..?? no no: per uint32 read = 4 consecutive d (d = 4b..4b+3): byte b: lo nibble → q_abs[h, 2·j2] += w_lo · qs[d_b]; hi → dot_hi += w_hi · qs[d_b]: so per uint32: 8 FMA total across 2 dots. END: q_abs[h][2·j2] = dot_lo − zq; [2·j2+1] = dot_hi − zq ✓ (fp32 store). CORRECTNESS-check: k_nope in ref = GEMV c_kv@W with c_kv bf16: our q-side formulation absorbs ✓ (algebraic identity). WAIT — s,z for kv_b: G4 = 4 groups along in(512): group g = (2·j2)/128 = j2/64 ✓ chunk 32 rows → g = jc/2 ✓ s slice [g, h·256 + d] ✓ z same ✓. rope-q items: 4 items × 512 elems: elems index e: h = e/64, i = e%64: pair index ii = i/2: even: out = q[2i]-cosθ·... : x = SC_MLAQ[h·192 + 128 + i]: even e2: re = x2·cos_i − x2p1·sin_i (i = ii... cos = COSC[pos][ii] with pos = L Call.L ✓. round bf16 → write SC_QR fp32?? attention scores: ref uses fp32(roped bf16) ✓ store fp32(round) — SC_QR fp32 2048 ✓. kvrow item: c_kv new row: 512 elems: v = bf16(SC_KV[e]) → WSC[L·512 + e] (bf16) ✓; k_rope: 64 elems: k = bf16(SC_KV[512+i]) rope → bf16 → WSK[L·64 + i] ✓ (use COSC/SINC[L]). copy items (fresh): row r in 0..L-1: 512 c + 64 k: WSC[r·512+e] = ckv_src[r·512+e]; WSK[...] = krc_src[...] — plain bf16 copies ✓. --- /dev/null
+++ b//ephemeral/mega-outputs/runs/20260716_091712_kinetic-claude_kinetic-0715_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/mega_impl.py
@@ -0,0 +1,1073 @@
+"""Build + cache the CUDA megakernel extension for solution.py.
+
+Single __global__ kernel: 4-block decode step (KDA x3, MLA x1, each + MoE).
+Cooperative launch, custom 2-word grid barriers, dynamic atomic work counters.
+
+Weight layout (flat blobs, produced by solution.Model.prepare):
+  WB (uint8): per piece, packed int4 rows (in//2, out) row-major.
+  SB (bf16) : per piece, [scales(G,N) flat][zeros(G,N) flat], G = in//128.
+              expert pieces are ex-major: scales (E,G,N) flat then zeros (E,G,N).
+  AB (bf16) : norms, router (64,2304), beta (32,2304), conv (3,4096,4).
+Offset vector order (LayV):
+  wb[bi*11+p], sb[bi*11+p] with pieces p:
+    KDA: 0 q,1 k,2 v,3 g,4 o,5 eg,6 eu,7 ed,8 sg,9 su,10 sd
… +1060 more lines

The file got too long to hold in my head while writing — the right move is to restructure: I'll rewrite mega_impl.py cleanly with a systematic structure, and importantly, simplify some designs: 1. The bi==0 residual bug: gemv MODE 1 reads `res` fp32. For bi==0, residual source is x_in (bf16). Solution: make P2 residual always from a fp32 "x current" scratch: at block start (in stage_xn time? no...). Simplest: kernel keeps `SC_XCUR` fp32: at block start, if bi==0, convert x_in → SC_XCUR... but which phase? The stage_xn phase BEFORE P0's gemv: stage_xn could also write xcur. x is needed at P2 (oproj residual) and... Actually simpler: P2 gemv MODE 1T templated on dtype: res ptr either bf16 or fp32. Make MODE 1 always bf16-residual from... h_acc consumers later round anyway. Convert: for bi==0 res=x_in (bf16) with separate code path in the sink (template MODE 3 = residual-bf16). Clean: template `<int MODE, typename RT>`. Hmm wait, actually cleaner: at P0 time (stage_xn), additionally write SC_XCUR fp32 = x (bf16-rounded). The CTA does it as stage work. xcur used for oproj residual. For blocks>0, xcur = moeacc (already fp32, no copy needed — point at it). But P0's stage also needs... xn stage reads x_in per-CTA anyway; write xcur ONCE per... per-CTA redundant writes of same values — benign race (same values)? It's a benign duplicate store. But cleaner: sink template handles bf16 residual directly. Let me do template on residual type: `template <int MODE> gemv_tile(..., const void* res, bool res_bf16)`. Actually simplest: add MODE 3 = residual from bf16 pointer. Fine. 2. gu_tile placeholder — delete, keep gu_tile2 renamed to gu_tile, but it uses `__shared__ float redu2[NWARP][128]` (8KB static) — static shared + dynamic union... static __shared__ inside template function is OK; but it reduces available smem... 8KB static + 25KB dyn — fine (max ~227K, occupancy-limited by regs anyway). Wait — static shared memory inside a __device__ function: allowed (function-scope static shared). OK but it adds 8KB static everywhere... let me instead use the SmGemv.xsu area? xsu is 128 floats — need 16×128 more floats for second reduce. Better: since gu needs TWO reductions, and red area [16][128] = 2048 floats... I can reduce accg first into red, read g, then store accu and reduce again (two syncthreads) — avoids second buffer: - write accg to red, sync, each col-lane reads g total, keep in reg, sync (barrier before overwrite), write accu to red, sync, read u total, compute silu. That's 4 syncthreads — fine. 3. `stage_x(smg_noop_unused)` typo — remove; the HID xsum staging after stage_xn inside P0 is needed (stage_xn only stages xn; xsu must also be computed). Rework: after stage_xn, call a helper `compute_xsu` that fills smg.xsu for HID. stage_xn handles rstd + xn; separate helper for xsu of arbitrary len. Let me define clean helper set: - `stage_xn(D, smg, xb, xf, nrm, sm_red)` → rstd; stages xn (HID) rounded; then xsu (HID/64 units) computed inside (needs another loop over 36 units — fold in). - `stage_raw(smg, src, len)` → stages len fp32 + xsu. 4. moe_phase device function — needed, referenced. Since KDA and MLA MoE phases are identical except counters, make a real __device__ function. 5. In the P1 qabs: `zq` shared race nonsense, clean with sm_red. 6. In rope: COSC layout [LMAX][32] fp32 (my table is (LMAX, 32) — 32 = QKR/2 pairs ✓). 7. P2 attention: `sm.a.c` is [16][512] bf16 = 16KB ✓. Staging loop `e < np*(KVL/2)` — KVL/2 = 256 uint32 per row ✓. 8. Need P8 only after last block; also after KDA block 2's P4 there's a gsync (fine). 9. IMPORTANT BUG in my skeleton: `-- P3 MoE gate/up` counter id `4 + bi*3` and `5 + bi*3` — MLA uses 6... let me systematize counter ids: each phase needs a unique work counter: block bi KDA: P0 cid=0, P1=1, P2=2, P3=3, P4=4 for all? NO — counters zeroed per launch end; each phase has unique counter within launch: cid allocation: global enumerate: P0=0, P1=1, P2=2, P3=3, P4=4 per block ×4 blocks = 20 + MLA extra: MLA P0..P7: 8 ids + P8: total: KDA 5×3 = 15, MLA 9 (P0..P7, P8) = 24 → use cid = phaseglobal enumerated sequentially: KDA blocks: 0..4, 5..9, 10..14; MLA: 15..23; P8: 23? let me assign: counter = running index: KDA block i: cid0 = i*5. MLA: base 15: P0=15,...,P7=22, P8=23. ✓ 24 counters ≤ 64 ✓. 10. Barrier slots: KDA: 5 per block × 3 = 15; MLA: 8; plus the final P8 pre-sync... wait I put `gsync(slot++)` before P8 in my draft — P8 must come after the LAST P7's gsync; the P7 gsync IS that barrier; P8 reads MOACC — safe; then NO extra gsync needed after P7: P8 follows directly. But my draft had P7 gsync via moe_phase slot+=2 then a separate gsync before P8 — that's ONE extra barrier (needed? P7's gsync happens inside moe_phase; the extra one before P8 is REDUNDANT but harmless... remove it: P8 immediately after moe_phase's P7 gsync ✓ correct synchronization (MOACC complete). Saves a slot. Total slots: 15 + 8 = 23 ≤ 31 ✓. Hmm wait — but inside moe_phase, the LAST gsync is after P7 (counter check). Let me recount moe_phase syncs: KDA: sync after P3(gu), sync after P4(down) = 2 syncs within moe_phase. MLA moe_phase (P6,P7): 2 syncs. So ALL blocks: KDA: P0,P1,P2 syncs (3) + moe 2 = 5 ✓; MLA: P0,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 (6) + moe 2 = 8 ✓ total 23 ✓ then P8 no barrier needed after ✓. 11. One more REAL bug class: within gemv_tile the smem reduce `smg.red` — but multiple gemv_tile calls may happen per phase per CTA sequentially; fine. 12. The xsum staging: `stage_xn` stages xn; I need xsu for HID (36 units). For the KDA P0 and P3 and MLA P0/P6 phases. stage_raw for o (64 units) and moe_h (16 units). Write one `compute_xsu(smg, units)`. 13. Register pressure in P2 attention: acc[2][16] = 32 + q pointers + misc — OK. 14. Potential correctness landmine: In gemv phases for KDA P0, the value stored MUST be fp32 raw dot with rstd applied — but ALSO NOTE the vs. reference issue: reference GEMV output is bf16! P1 rounds to bf16 anyway (conv/g). ✓. But is rstd applied at store correct given sinks: sink y[cg] = v*rstd — reference rounds the bf16 OUTPUT; our fp32 store keeps more bits; P1 rounds → bf16(fp32 val) == ref bf16 value modulo accumulation-order diffs ✓ fine. 15. Attention q_abs values: NOT rstd-scaled — q GEMV applied rstd at store (SC_MLAQ = rstd·...) ✓ then qabs multiplies by W (kv_b dequant) — matches ref (ref: q = q_proj(x) bf16 → float; kv_b on c_kv... ref materializes kvb = c_kv@Wkv_b per token and scores q_nope·k_nope: absorb identity Σ_d q_d·(Σ_j W[j,d]·c[l,j]) = Σ_j (Σ_d q_d W[j,d])·c[l,j] = Σ_j q_abs[j]·c: EXACT identity (up to fp reassociation) ✓ no rstd issue. 16. stage_raw for P2 (o vector): SC_O is fp32(rounded bf16) ✓ input to oproj: reference rounds o to bf16 BEFORE o_proj ✓ ours already rounded at store ✓. 17. Down-phase MOEH read: `stage_x(sm.g, MOEH + j*MINT, MINT)` per ITEM — stages 1024 + xsu. OK. But careful: stage_x takes `const float* src` and `SmGemv&` — inside a while loop with next_work's __syncthreads — consistent across threads ✓. 18. The gemv_tile sink write for MODE0 with masked cols: for kva tiles cols 512..639 (N=576): writing SC_KV buffer: sized 640 ✓ safe. 19. `next_work` — uses `atomicAdd` ull returns ull ✓. 20. Softplus: reference `-F.softplus(g)` then `.exp()`: gexp = exp(-softplus(g)) = exp(-log1p(exp(g))): my `gg = -__logf(1+__expf(gg))` → PRECISION DANGER: for g>~10, expf(g) overflows → logf(inf)=inf → -inf → exp→0 OK; for g very negative: expf(g)~0 → logf(1)=0 → -0 → exp = 1 ✓; but for g ~ 15-88: expf(30) = 1e13 fine fp32 (max 3.4e38); g_proj outputs |g| ~ small anyway. torch softplus: log1p(exp(x)) with threshold 20 → x if x>20. My version loses precision for x>20: expf(x) large, logf(expf(x)) = x - small → fine actually. Precision OK-ish. Better: gexp = exp(-softplus(g)): compute softplus properly with __logf(1+__expf(-|g|)) + max(g,0). I'll do that stable version. 21. `stage_xn` signature has xn_out_src_b16 unused — drop. 22. The `qs` shared arrays inside the qabs loop are declared `__shared__` inside a while loop → fine (static shared, same storage). 23. `sm_red` conflict: shared between stage_xn reduce and other uses — sequential only ✓. 24. P1 conv: `wnd` pointer const cast for the shift — shift is in P2 with bf16* cast ✓. 25. conv_w layout: conv_w (3, C, 4): conv_w[idx][c][j]: index = qv*C*4 + c*4 + j: j=0..3 with j=0 being OLDEST: ref: w = conv_w[idx].float().transpose(0,1) — wait ref code: `w = self.conv_w[idx].float().transpose(0, 1); out = (win.float() * w).sum(0)`: conv_w[idx] is (C, 4); win is (4, C). transpose(0,1) of (C,4) → (4, C). So out[c] = Σ_j win[j,c]*convw[c,j] where win[0] = oldest = prev row 0. In my kernel: val (current) pairs with cw[c*4+3] (j=3) ✓ oldest wnd[0] with cw[c*4+0] ✓ — wait win = cat([prev(3), val]): win[0]=prev[0], win[1]=prev[1], win[2]=prev[2], win[3]=val. So Σ_j win[j]·w[c,j] = prev0·w[c,0] + prev1·w[c,1] + prev2·w[c,2] + val·w[c,3]. My code: `o = val*cw[c*4+3]; o += wnd[0]*cw[c*4+2]; o += wnd[1]*cw[c*4+1]; o += wnd[2]*cw[c*4+0]` — WRONG pairing! Should be wnd[0]*cw[0], wnd[1]*cw[1], wnd[2]*cw[2], val*cw[3]. My pairing reversed! Fix: o += wnd[j*CC + c] * cw[c*4 + j] for j=0,1,2 and val*cw[c*4+3]. 26. Another important detail: gemv_tile reads `smg.xsu[u]` for units — xsu computed from `xn` by compute_xsu(smg, units∙). P2 attention uses sm.a.c[16][512] bf16 = 16KB ✓ fine. Let me add __syncthreads() at start of compute_xsu. Another bug: in `moe_phase`, stage_xn is called with `xb=nullptr, xf=D.SC+SC_HACC` ✓ then compute_xsu ✓. Bug in gu_tile: after the first reduce read (`gv`), I never put up a barrier to protect smg.red overwrite?? Sequence: write accg→red, sync, l<8 reads gv from red, sync, write accu→red, sync, read uv. The middle sync: after reading gv, before writing accu — needed ✓ I have `__syncthreads()` between (the "second pass for u" comment) ✓ yes there is one. In gemv_tile MODE sink: `if (l < 8)`: int col = (threadIdx.x>>5)*8 + l — for wid in 0..15, l<8: col = wid*8 + l ∈ [0,128) ✓. Bug check in P1 qabs: `qs[d]` staging uses `sm_red[threadIdx.x >> 5]` for warp partial sums — threadIdx.x<128 → warps 0..3 ✓ sm_red[0..3] ✓ overlap with zqv... fine. `zq` subtract: SC_QABS[h*512 + 2*j2] = dlo − zq — sc_qabs values... let me sanity check the math: q_abs[h, j] = Σ_d (w_jd − z_gd)·s_gd·q_d where j = 2·j2 or 2·j2+1; my dlo = Σ_d w_lo·qs_d where qs_d = q_d·s_d. zq = Σ_d z_d·qs_d. So q_abs[h,2·j2] = dlo − zq ✓ y = Σ (w_lo)·qs − z·qs = Σ (w−z)·s·q ✓. But wait, kv_b groups: in=512, groups of 128 → 4 groups: j2 row covers j=2·j2, 2·j2+1: group g = (2·j2)//128 = j2//64. jc = chunk of 32 j2 → covers j in 32·jc·2 .. = 64·jc..64·jc+63: group = (64·jc)/128 = jc/2 ✓ g = jc>>1 ✓. Hmm wait: SC_QABS + h*KVL + 2*j2: KVL = 512 ✓ j2 in [0,256): 2·j2+1 < 512 ✓. s index: `D.SB[D.sb[wbbase+2] + g*(NMH*256) + h*256 + d]`: NMH·256 = 8192 = N ✓ zeros at sb + 4·8192 + g·8192 + ... ✓ 4 groups ✓. Bug: `float s = b2f(D.SB[...])` — single bf16 load per d (2B loads — fine). In P2's attention: the kr staging index `e < np*(QKR/2)` — QKR/2 = 32 uint32 per row ✓. Attention `d = (d + dr)` — WAIT: `d` after first reduce is the c-dot from lane0 only (shfl_down leaves result in lane 0 but other lanes hold partial sums!). I then compute dr with full reduce, then `d = (d + dr)*MLASCALE; d = shfl(d,0)`. BUG: after the first reduce, only lane 0 has the full sum d; after second reduce only lane 0 has full dr. `d + dr` on lane 0 = correct total; then shfl broadcast from lane 0 ✓ correct. OK fine. BUT — `__shfl_down_sync` with mask 0xffffffff requires all 32 lanes participating — inside `if` free code path... in attention it's main flow ✓. For P4 oabs: `if (threadIdx.x < 32)` — `__syncwarp()` inside, syncthreads at end by all — ✓. `csum` shared: written by lane<8 within `if (threadIdx.x<32)` — only warp 0 — but `__syncwarp()` syncs warp 0 ✓. In oabs, xl/xh read `SC + SC_CTX + h*KVL + 2*j2` — j2 over 0..255 ✓. csum[u] = sum of ctx over unit's 64 j values: unit u covers j = 64u..64u+63: `for (j = lane*64; j < lane*64+64; j++)` for lane<8 ✓ (lane u → csum[u]) ✓. +"""Debug: run one megakernel step in a subprocess; if it hangs, read BAR state."""
+import sys
+
+import torch
+
+sys.path.insert(0, ".")
+
+
+def main():

The S update ORDER vs reference: S = S*exp(g); pred = Σ S·k; S += β·k(v−pred); o = Σ S·q. Mine: sv = S*exp ✓, pr = Σ sv·kc ✓, snew = sv + β*kc*vp ✓ o += snew*qc ✓. 3. gexp: reference g.exp() where g = -softplus(g_proj(x)): so exp(-softplus(g)) ✓ mine ✓. 4. beta: ref beta = sigmoid(beta_proj(x).float()): β = sigmoid(βw@xn): computed in P0 side-item as Σ xn·bw... MISSING rstd multiply!!! In P0 side-item: `SC[SC_BETA + h] = s` where s = Σ xn[k]·bw[h,k] — but xn is the NORMED input WITHOUT rstd (xn = x*normw, scaled by rstd pulled out!). The beta GEMV needs rstd·Σ — I FORGOT THE RSTD FACTOR in the side-item!!! β = sigmoid(rstd·s + ...) — currently sigmoid(s) with s = Σ xn·bw and NOT rstd-scaled → β wrong by huge factor (rstd ~ 1/(|x|/48) ~ 20x!!). Fix: SC[SC_BETA + h] = s * rstd. The side-item has access to... rstd computed in stage_xn by EVERY CTA — the specific CTA doing the side-item has rstd in scope (P0's `float rstd` is in scope inside the while loop ✓ accessible ✓). Reference beta_proj: nn.Linear: input x bf16 → cublas → bf16 OUTPUT rounded, then sigmoid(float()) — should I round? sigmoid(bf16(rstd·s)) — add rounding: f2b then back: minor. Add for fidelity. 5. The o = Σ snew·qc — qc has DKSCALE applied ✓. 6. pred uses sm.s.aux — fixed size 32 ✓. Then dbg=2 should show better. Unwrap the cosine: cos(A,B) = Σa·b/(|a||b|). If solution rows = init rows except ~2% of VALUES wrong... e.g., rope on the NEW row k_rope wrong — k_rope compared separately? No, only c_kv. UNLESS THE GEMV ITSELF IS WRONG in a systematic way that ALSO corrupts... the copies don't involve GEMV. So 0.978 means rows don't match REF rows: because reference's cache contains rows = init_state EXACTLY... mine = init_state (copy) EXACTLY... cos(A, B) where A=B except last row garbage-ish: = (S_ab + δ)/ (sqrt(S_aa)·sqrt(S_bb+δ2)) where S_ab = Σ copies a·b = |rows|². ≈ |rows|²/(|a|·|b|) with |a|≈|b|≈|rows| → ≈ |rows|²/(|rows|²·(1+ small)) ≈ 0.9995. So 0.978 implies |a| or |b| LARGER: |b|² = |rows|² + |garbage|²: |garbage| ~ norm of new row... all rows are ~ N(0, 0.1)·sqrt(512): |new| similar magnitude. cos ≈ |rows|²/(|rows|·sqrt(|rows|²+|garbage|²)) = 1/sqrt(1+|g|²/|rows|²) ≈ 1/sqrt(1+1/2048)≈0.9998. STILL high. So 0.978 can't come from one bad row!! Something systematic: e.g., my ws contains rows of a PREVIOUS dbg run's c_kv?! st_s fresh → copy SHOULD overwrite... UNLESS fresh copy items were capped: `NT = 261 + (cl.fresh ? cl.L : 0)` — with L=2048: NT=2309 items — but the WORK COUNTER 16 is reset only at prepare... I loop with sol._bar.zero_() each dbg run ✓. Ours: from OUR kv_a GEMV from OUR input. The input to MLA differs because KDA outputs differ (beta bug + maybe more) → our new row DIFFERS from ref's → ~random δ. As computed, cos should remain 0.9998. 0.978?! Unless our new row has HUGE magnitude (broken GEMV → giant values → δ dominates |b|): cos ≈ |rows|²/(|rows|·|giant|) → could be 0.978 if |new_row| ~ 5×|rows|. AND gemv sinks multiply by rstd. One or the other — not both! Router: reference router input = bf16 normed → logits = xn@W with xn including rstd ✓ so in moe_phase, xn already includes rstd3 → atomicAdd(SC_LOGIT+cc2, s) (no rstd3!). silu gu: same pass rstd3 → pass 1.f.

The ONLY differences: (a) input staging (xf/MOACC vs x_in) and (b) TIMING/state of the machine (later in the launch). (a)'s mechanism: stage_xn reads xf[k] with b2f(f2b(xf[k]))... MOACC deterministic ✓ so stage output deterministic... — UNLESS: **the stage_xn for block-1 races with something writing MOACC?!** What writes MOACC concurrently with block-1 P0?? The block-0 P4 down-gemv finished at barrier 4 (before P0 block-1)... hmm — THE ATOMICADDs from block-0's P4 must ALL BE VISIBLE — the barrier guarantees... WAIT A SECOND. THE BARRIER: blocks' boundaries: after block-0's moe_phase, we do `GSYNC()` — the down phase's atomicAdds are in-flight writes ACCELERATED by... they're device-scope atomics to L2 — before the gsync: each CTA's __threadfence?? My gsync: CTA's thread0 does syncthreads (block memory fence), atomicAdd (arrive), release/spin. The arrival's `atomicAdd(arr, 1)` — does it ORDER the CTA's prior DEVICE writes (atomicAdds to MOACC) such that other CTAs see them after passing the barrier?? For the CUDA memory model: the arriving atomicAdd should have RELEASE semantics for cross-CTA visibility!!! My atomicAdd in CUDA C (atom.add.relaxed.device) is RELAXED — it does NOT order prior writes!!! So a CTA's float atomicAdds to SC_MOACC (or float stores to SC_QKVG, S states, etc.) may linger in L2/local fabric and other CTAs passing the barrier may read STALE values!!! THE BARRIER HAS NO MEMORY FENCE — THIS IS THE BUG!!! In CUDA, atomic ops are relaxed; __threadfence() is needed before the arrive (to make prior writes visible) and after the spin (acquire). The classic pattern: threadfence → atomicAdd(arrive) → spin → threadfence. I missed both threadfences! Why did block 0 work? Mostly gets lucky (delays short); deeper in the launch, races widen. FIX: in gsync: `__threadfence();` before atomicAdd(arrive) (after syncthreads covering block-level), and `__threadfence();` after spin before syncthreads. Also in the ROUTER flag: writer already has __threadfence ✓; spinners lack a post-spin threadfence → add. Same in `next_work`? The counter values flow only scheduling info ✓ no data-ordering needs. But hmm — this also races cross-launch: P8 xout write + counter zeroing — covered by gsync ✓ +final zeroing after gsync ✓ covered (threadfence in the last gsync covers xout visibility... the kernel-end makes all writes visible to host via launch completion ✓). Also the flag: spinners proceed to read SC_IDS — add threadfence. This explains every symptom beautifully: - Non-deterministic block-1 GEMV (stale MOACC reads → some columns get stale/garbage xn... wait actually STALE MOACC = block-0's partially-updated values = "wrong but from valid memory" ✓ matches the partial-value error magnitude!) - The rstd from stale values ✓ matches - The NaN/garbage-ids → logits from stale xn → NaN?? (stale float values could be NaN bit patterns from DIFFERENT SC regions!? SC_MOACC's memory contained older data — old QABS values etc. — could be anything including patterns that read as NaN floats!!! ✓ explains garbage ids = -1 → NaN → bi2. Patch now.

Apply this in the residual sink: y = (res) + bf16(v) — but h should also be bf16-rounded at the end (x + attnout is bf16 + bf16 → torch casts to bf16!). The full chain: h = bf16( bf16(x) + bf16(v) )... Since block output h is used bf16-CAST later anyway, match: HACC = bf16(x + bf16(v))... but storing bf16 loses precision for my fp32 MOACC... Since matching torch's h exactly → router inputs identical → ids identical, then MoE inputs identical → outputs identical... the whole chain bit-matches torch except in places where I *choose* fp32 over bf16. So the general PRINCIPLE: **Round every intermediate to bf16 exactly where torch's reference implementation materializes a bf16 tensor.** Every torch op that produces a bf16 tensor is a rounding checkpoint: - normed x (rmsnorm out): ✓ already rounding in stage_xn. - q/k/v/g proj outs: torch bf16 → round at P0 sink!! I currently DON'T round (fp32 in scratch); the conv rounds them when reading — v/k/q rounded at conv ✓ g rounded at gexp ✓ so the VALUES used downstream ARE rounded ✓ equivalent... but beta uses xn not these ✓ fine. - conv outs: rounded ✓; S fp32 ✓ same as torch (torch S fp32 throughout!) ✓; o rounded ✓. - **attn/o_proj out: ROUND to bf16** ← FIX #1. Then h = x + that: reference h is bf16 (add of two bf16 → bf16 tensor) → storing bf16(bf16(x) + bf16(v)): with HACC fp32 buffer: HACC = f32(bf16(f32(bf16(x)) + f32(bf16(v))))?? bf16 add of bf16+bf16: torch: produces bf16 tensor from bf16 inputs (exact fp32 accumulation internally then one rounding) → value = bf16(f32(x_bf16) + f32(v_bf16)) ✓ apply at sink: t = b2f(f2b(b2f(f2b(res)) + b2f(f2b(v)))). - router logits: torch Linear → bf16 → round ✓ applied already in topk. - MoE: gate/up/down: torch: `_ffn`: x.float() @ W — the matmul is FP32, NO intermediate rounding!! silu fp32, product fp32, down fp32, weights fp32, out fp32 → `out.to(bf16)` at moe return ONLY. So MOEH fp32 ✓ correct; delta accumulate fp32 ✓; final y rounded at moe return ✓. BUT the summation of moe contributions: torch: out fp32 += w_j × ffn_j — sequential per expert in index order — mine: atomicAdd random order — fp32 order diffs ~ 1e-6 (fine, and total is rounded at end ✓). - Block out: h + moe → bf16 rounding: **round SC_MOACC's final value to bf16 as the block output** — WAIT the residual: y = h + moeout: torch: bf16 tensor h + bf16 tensor moeout → bf16 out. My MOACC accumulates h (fp32 w/ round-at-use) + Σ deltas fp32 → then P8/next-stage rounds. To match: MOACC_final = bf16(bf16(h) + bf16(moeout_fp32_sum))? torch: moeout = bf16(sum_j ffn parts accumulated fp32 → one rounding!!) — i.e., moe_delta = torch's moe step output = f32(sum) → bf16. Then blockout = bf16(h_bf16 + delta_bf16). So: my delta accum = f32 moeout-unrounded ✓ accumulate fp32 ✓ then: MOACC_new = bf16(bf16(h) + bf16(delta_total)). And delta's own components: Σ_j w_j·ffn_j accumulated fp32 — torch accumulates `out + w*ffn` per expert sequentially (fp32 add with w multiplies) — mine sums via fp32 atomicAdd of w-scaled deltas ✓ same amount, order diffs only. - But CAREFUL: my MOACC also serves as the residual for the NEXT block's P0 staging AND the P2 oproj residual — both should read the ROUNDED value ✓ consistent.   • Round o_proj output and residual-add to bf16 at sinks
  • Block output = bf16(HACC + bf16(DELTA)) exactly like torch's two-step rounding

Experiment: switch __expf → expf and __logf → logf (accurate intrinsics) — sqrt in everything... let me just try NO fast-math but careful intrinsic selection: expf vs __expf: in CUDA without fast-math, expf() calls the ACCURATE implementation; __expf() is the fast one. I'm USING __expf/__logf — switch to expf/logf everywhere (exp, log1p...). silu: x/(1+exp(-x)) — 1+exp(-x) with expf then DIVIDE — reference uses torch.silu compute ~ similar. Similarly rsqrtf → use 1.0f/sqrtf(x) (IEEE round) vs torch.rsqrt... torch.rsqrt — likely accurate to ~1ulp. The rstd then multiplies every input element with systematic bias... use precise division. ACCEPT absorb noise (block 3 inputs are also near-ties, but with reduced intrinsic noise maybe tolerable). Now the FINAL: `best = shfl(best, 0); bi2 = shfl(bi2, 0)` — broadcast from LANE 0, NOT an actual reduce!! LANE 0's best is whatever lane 0 had after rounds of shifting: lane 0's partners: lanes 16, 8, 4, 2, 1 — at o=1: lane 0 receives lane 1's (X, 3): if X > best_lane0 → lane 0 takes (X, 3)!!! → bi2 = 3 broadcast!!! Wait but lane 0 initially: best = max(p0=e0 prob, p1=e1 prob of lane 0 = experts 0,1: small values). After o=16: receives lane 16's (X, 32): X > small → lane 0 = (X, 32)! After o=8: receives lane 8's best — if lane 8's is smaller, keeps (X,32). o=4: keeps (X, 32). o=2: keeps. o=1: receives lane 1's (X, 3): X > X is FALSE → keeps (X, 32)!!! → bi2 = 32!!! THERE IT IS: the tie-break depends on the shfl-tree order — lane 16's candidate reaches lane 0 BEFORE lane 1's! The rule ends up "higher lane wins some ties" — arbitrary tree order. torch.topk evidently picked 3 (lower EXPERT index). FIX: implement tie-break preferring LOWER EXPERT INDEX explicitly: in reduce: `if (ob > best || (ob == best && oi < bi2))`. ob == best with oi < bi2 → take lower index. That's deterministic lower-index-wins. Then for EXACT ties: matches torch IF torch prefers lower index... check.py itself will tell. This deterministic rule only matters for EXACT bf16 ties (like this one). Torch CUB topk for ties... honestly unknown — but this specific case wants lower index (3 over 32). Let me just try prefer-lowest and see if it aligns with torch on all checks. Caveat: probs computed from logits that COULD be legitimately ordered differently from torch's bf16 logits (as analyzed) — those remain flips regardless. But EXACT-bf16-ties now resolve toward torch's (presumed) rule. Apply: `if (ob > best || (ob == best && oi < bi2))`. There could be an issue when bi2 = -1 initially: ob == best == -1 with oi=-1: -1 < -1 false ✓ fine. But when best is big & ob == best & oi = -1?? oi=-1 is never paired (oi only -1 when best=-1 ✓ fine).

Actually the cleanest: **reduce THR to 256 and set maxrregcount=168** (256×168 = 43K, or uncapped: 165 regs × 256 = 42,240 ≤ 64K → occ=1 without spills!). At 256 threads the barrier is a bit faster; per-CTA work needs rebalancing: fewer warps per CTA → more K-split per warp (36 units over 8 warps → 4-5 units each — more per-warp work, similar total). But fewer threads/CTA might reduce streaming concurrency per SM?! Each SM has only 256 threads: outstanding loads potential halves unless ILP improves. Risky. Loop per column per lane: ~2.25 uint4-LDG + 72 FFMA+LOP3×… ≈ 2.25 mem-ops, ~ 110 ALU ops → ALU:MEM = 50:1 — heavily ALU-bound per lane but warps in a CTA are plentiful (16) so ALU throughput totals: let me recount per column: 128 lanes?? per column total instr = 72 uint4 loads + 8*8 LOP3 + 72 FSUB + 72 FMA ×?? per column (K=2304): weights 2304: FMA 2304/warp ÷32 = 72 per lane ✓ FSUB same, SHF/LOP3 ~ 288/warp ÷ 32 = 9... FINE. Then per-tile-assignment: warp = (matrix, col-chunk of say 8 columns): to keep lanes' x slices: COLUMN LOOP over 8 cols before reassignment... acc4 reduce after column → store col result. Load balance: total warp-items = columns/8 × matrices ✓ scale... fine. Occupancy: x-regs 32 + acc ~12 + addressing ~60-80 total regs → more CTAs possible?? The point: this should stream at near-peak: per-lane 16B loads with no cross-lane sharing and coalescing... WAIT — coalescing ACROSS THE WARP: lane reads ((uint4*)colbase)[lane]: addresses = colbase + lane·16: CONTIGUOUS 512B per warp ✓ ✓ perfect. Then next iteration index+32: another 512B ✓. This is essentially the WARP-STREAM pattern. The x-slice smem/global-read problem vanishes via x-REGISTERS across columns. OK — but there is a catch for SMALL N matrices: kv_a (576 cols) → each column still full work ✓ fine. And GROUPS: the k-group for lane ly's weights: lane ly covers k=32*ly..32*ly+31: group = (32·ly)//128 = ly//4 → lane's (s,z) per chunk: all weights in a lane's uint4 share the group (32 k-values = 1/4 group ✓ exactly one group!) ✓ s,z = SMEM or global broadcast per (group, col): per column-lane-idx... per uint4: lane needs s for group ly//4?? within uint4 same group ✓ so per column: lane reads s,z (groups gy = ly//4: same for all its 2-3 chunks ✓) = 2 bf16 loads per column per lane (sector-shared with 3 other lanes ✓ or L1-broadcast). The z-correction: y = Σ_g [s_g·dot_g − s_g·z_g·xsum_g] — per lane-partial: y_lane = s_gl·(dot − z·xsum_gl) → after lane's chunks done: y_lane = s·dot − (s·z)·xsum: xsum = Σ lane's x regs = precomputed per lane at stage! Then per column: v = Σ_lanes y_lane via shuffle ✓. NICE — actually I realize this eliminates the acc/partials entirely per unit. Hmm, wait — xsum precomputed per lane per x-stage: fine. And this GEMV has NO smem reduce, NO syncthreads inside!! Per-column sync-free; store direct to scratch!! Also for the GU (gate/up) it's 2 columns in parallel — or just process them as separate column items ✓ silu at store ✓. For split-K situations (o_proj N small): K per column is the loop — fine (never needed split-K). So the new GEMV everywhere, column-per-warp. Per CTA: 16 warps work independently → work unit = (matrix, col-octet?) — warp takes ONE column at a time via the DYNAMIC counter handed out per CTA!? Warps need their own work ids — dynamic atomic per warp?? cost: atomic per warp-item = thousands of atomics... warp work ids from a WARP-LEVEL dynamic counter: each warp atomicAdd on its own — 1824 warps × ~1 atomic per item... ~ items = total columns/8 ≈ few thousand — atomics fine. But non-deterministic ≥. Alternatively static assignment: warp w of CTA p handles column-items w*G + p... stride grid·16: FULLY static: item = g_warp; item += 16·114... = static ✓ deterministic ✓ and ragged-tail acceptable for many-items phases ✓ SIMPLE — do STATIC for warps, keep atomic per CTA only for coarse phases? simpler: make ALL phases warp-strided static work assignment over their own item lists (replacing every dynamic counter!). Then delete all atomics & counters!!! Wait — but tail balance... for phases with few items (o_proj 36 col-octets 288 columns → 144 warp-items?): K=4096: warp-items = 36 → 36/1824 = %2 utilization again?! For SMALL phases use PER-LANE items even (column = lane item!): o_proj: 2304 cols / (32 lanes×...): warp-item = 4 cols (each lane 4B chunks... hmm no— design said warp-col each 16B... with per-lane columns (each lane its own column with 16B loads?) — lanes read non-coalesced but 100%-sector-util-ish (16B of 32B sectors = 50%): fine for small phases. OK decision framework: - BIG phases (weights ≥ 1MB): warp-per-column with x-regs static arrangement. - SMALL phases (≤ 18 tiles): use the same warp-per-column but more items (column = item, or col-pair) → 2304/64=... o_proj: 2304 cols: warp-items with 2 cols per warp = 1152 items ✓ good util. - The small phases (attention etc) unchanged.
